title: The Secret to Marathon-Winning Humanoid Robots
blurb: The Honor Lightning humanoid ran a half-marathon in 50 minutes and 26 seconds on April 19, 2026, beating the human world record by 7 minutes and last year's best robot time by nearly two hours. An IEEE Spectrum analysis finds no exotic breakthrough behind the result, just careful drivetrain engineering, with optimal gearing around 45:1 keeping knee-motor heat near 150W and avoiding the overheating that forced rivals like Unitree to use ice backpacks.

title: Predicting model behavior before release by simulating deployment
blurb: OpenAI introduced Deployment Simulation, a method that replays past conversations in a privacy-preserving way against a candidate model to estimate how it will behave before release. Applied across GPT-5-series Thinking deployments, the technique improved estimates of undesired behavior rates, surfaced novel forms of misalignment, and reduced the chance that models could tell they were being tested.

title: Qwen-Robot Suite: A Foundation Model Suite for Physical World Intelligence
blurb: Alibaba's Qwen team released Qwen-Robot Suite, a set of foundation models aimed at physical-world intelligence for robotics. The suite targets perception, reasoning, and control so robots can interpret their surroundings and plan actions, extending Qwen's language and vision work into embodied systems.

title: Microsoft Confirms RoguePlanet Defender Zero-Day, Says Patch is in Development
blurb: Microsoft confirmed a Defender zero-day codenamed RoguePlanet, now tracked as CVE-2026-50656, a privilege-escalation flaw rated 7.8 that hands attackers a SYSTEM-level shell via a race condition. The company says a patch is in development, nearly a week after researcher Chaotic Eclipse released a proof-of-concept that reportedly works whether or not Defender's real-time protection is enabled.

title: Steam Workshop abused to spread malware via Wallpaper Engine app
blurb: Attackers abused Steam Workshop's Wallpaper Engine app to push DarkKomet backdoors, credential stealers, and cryptominers via malicious wallpaper packages.

title: Z.ai’s open-weights GLM-5.2 beats GPT-5.5 on multiple long-horizon coding benchmarks for 1/6th the cost
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, a 753B-parameter open-weights model under an unrestricted MIT license that beats GPT-5.5 on several long-horizon coding benchmarks at one-sixth the cost. Available now on Hugging Face, the Z.ai API, and 20-plus coding environments, it ships with a 1M-token context window and a new IndexShare architecture that cuts per-token compute by 2.9x at maximum length.

title: A near-autonomous AI chemist improves a challenging reaction in medicinal chemistry
blurb: OpenAI connected GPT‑5.4 to Molecule.one's Maria, an agentic chemistry system wired to a high-throughput lab, and tasked it with improving Chan–Lam coupling, a reaction used to form carbon-nitrogen bonds. The model independently targeted primary sulfonamides and proposed mild oxidants like TEMPO, lifting mean yield from 16.6% to 25.2% and improving results for over 80% of tested substrates.

title: Check out what's new in Android 17
blurb: Android 17 launched today, rolling out first to Pixel before reaching other eligible devices throughout 2026 with new productivity, gaming, and security features. Headline additions include Bubbles for floating any app into a compact window, Screen Reactions for combined selfie-and-screen recording, an optimized foldable gaming mode, and temporary precise-location and selective-contact sharing, with Gemini Intelligence coming to select advanced devices later this summer.

title: AMD silently removes memory encryption from consumer Ryzen CPUs, leaving users unaware that they may be vulnerable — security feature vanishes after newer AGESA firmware, AMD engineers go radio silent when pressed about the change
blurb: AMD has quietly stripped Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME), which encrypts RAM contents to block physical attacks, from consumer Ryzen CPUs outside its Pro lineup, apparently after the AGESA 1.2.7.0 firmware update. The change is undetectable on Windows and hard to spot on Linux, and AMD has neither acknowledged whether it was intentional nor explained why a feature that worked on consumer chips for years now appears Pro-only.

title: GLM-5.2 is the new leading open weights model on Artificial Analysis
blurb: Z.ai's GLM-5.2 is now the top open weights model on Artificial Analysis's Intelligence Index, scoring 51 to lead MiniMax-M3 and DeepSeek V4 Pro, both at 44. The MIT-licensed model keeps GLM-5.1's size and pricing while gaining most on scientific reasoning, expands its context window to 1M tokens, and lands on the intelligence-versus-cost Pareto frontier at roughly $0.46 per task.

title: Zama, Morpho and Steakhouse launch first ‘confidential DeFi yield’ vault on Ethereum
blurb: Zama, Morpho, and Steakhouse launched the first confidential DeFi yield vault on Ethereum, letting firms earn yield without exposing balances.

title: Massive breach spills credentials for thousands of sensitive networks
blurb: Researchers uncovered a breach of roughly 74,000 Fortinet firewalls, about half of all internet-facing FortiGate devices, exposing plaintext credentials for Oracle, Chevron, Lenovo, FedEx, a NATO defense contractor, and Fortinet itself. Russian-speaking attackers brute-forced login endpoints at scale and pivoted into Active Directory and Radius servers, and researchers say most compromised devices remained online with current, verified credentials.

title: FortiBleed leak exposes Fortinet VPN credentials for 73,000 devices.
blurb: A data leak dubbed FortiBleed has exposed Fortinet and FortiGate VPN credentials, including plaintext passwords, for 73,932 firewall URLs at organizations including Chevron, Samsung, Toyota, and AT&T. Researcher Bob Diachenko traced the cache to a Russian-speaking threat group that ran 1.16 billion credential attempts against FortiGate targets, cracked intercepted VPN hashes on a 45-GPU cluster, and moved laterally into internal Active Directory environments.

title: SpaceX to buy Cursor AI coding agent operator Anysphere for $60B
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire Anysphere, the maker of the Cursor AI coding agent, for $60B in its largest deal to date. The purchase pulls a leading AI-assisted development platform into Elon Musk's orbit, expanding SpaceX beyond aerospace and intensifying competition with GitHub Copilot and other coding-agent tools.

title: New research shows how AMIE, our medical AI, could help manage health conditions.
blurb: Google Research published a study in Nature showing AMIE, its medical AI system, can move beyond one-off diagnosis to long-term disease management by cross-referencing drug formularies and clinical guidelines using Gemini's long context. In a blinded study with patient actors, specialists found AMIE matched 21 primary care doctors on overall management reasoning and scored higher on plan precision and guideline alignment.

title: Building Reliable Agentic AI Systems
blurb: Martin Fowler's site published a case study on a system built with Bayer that lets pharmaceutical researchers query decades of study data buried in PDF reports. Author Sarang Sanjay Kulkarni traces its evolution from keyword search to an agentic research assistant that answers complex questions and drafts regulatory documents, detailing the reliability engineering required to make LLM outputs trustworthy.

title: Chrome and Firefox Updated to Patch Critical, High-Severity Vulnerabilities
blurb: Chrome and Firefox shipped updates patching over 70 vulnerabilities, including critical use-after-free bugs that could enable remote code execution.

title: Joomla, LiteSpeed Vulnerabilities Exploited in Attacks
blurb: Attackers are actively exploiting Joomla JCE (CVE-2026-48907) and LiteSpeed cPanel (CVE-2026-54420) flaws for code execution and root privilege escalation; patch immediately.

title: Google Gemini co-lead Noam Shazeer leaves for OpenAI
blurb: Noam Shazeer, Google vice president of engineering and a co-lead of its Gemini models, announced he is leaving to join OpenAI less than two years after returning to Google. His exit follows the 2024 deal that brought him back from Character.AI and lands as OpenAI, fresh off a confidential IPO filing, intensifies the industry-wide fight for top AI talent.

title: Introducing tfctl: The CLI for HCP Terraform and TFE
blurb: HashiCorp released tfctl, the first official CLI for HCP Terraform and Terraform Enterprise platform operations like canceling runs, managing workspaces, and administering organizations.

title: GrapheneOS has been ported to Android 17 and official releases are coming soon
blurb: GrapheneOS has been ported to Android 17 and tested on Pixel 6a, 7, 7a, 8, and the 10 series, with the first official release due a day after Android 17's launch. The privacy-focused Android fork is shipping a final Android 16 QPR2 build alongside the new port, and public testing across all supported devices begins immediately.

title: Show HN: cuTile Rust: Safe, data-race-free GPU kernels in Rust
blurb: cuTile Rust is an Nvidia research project for writing memory-safe, data-race-free GPU kernels in idiomatic Rust, extending ownership across the GPU launch boundary by partitioning mutable tensors into disjoint pieces while sharing immutable ones. A `#[cutile::module]` macro captures each kernel's AST and JIT-compiles it through CUDA Tile IR into a cubin, supporting synchronous launches, async pipelines, and CUDA graph replay.

title: The State of Fable, The Jailbreak Problem, SpaceX Acquires Cursor
blurb: Stratechery's latest Update argues the administration is likely wrong in its assessment of Fable, but contends that managing the fallout is ultimately Anthropic's responsibility rather than the government's. The piece also covers the broader jailbreak problem facing frontier labs and SpaceX's acquisition of Cursor, tying model safety obligations to the companies that build and ship the systems.

title: Tesco moving 40,000 server workloads off VMware amid Broadcom's “abusive conduct”
blurb: Tesco is migrating 40,000 server workloads off VMware, citing "abusive conduct" by Broadcom in a UK High Court breach-of-contract suit over licenses Tesco says it already paid for through 2026. The £73.7B retailer claims Broadcom cut off support in January and demanded duplicative subscriptions, forcing a costly migration to alternatives with reduced functionality that may not finish until the end of 2027.

title: Windows and Linux users: The deadline to update Secure Boot keys is near
blurb: Three Microsoft-signed certificates that anchor Secure Boot's chain of trust expire on June 24, requiring Windows and Linux users to update their cryptographic keys to keep verifying boot-time firmware and software. Without the update, systems lose protection against bootkits, firmware-level malware that loads before the OS and antivirus, survives reinstalls, and is notoriously hard to detect or remove.

title: RFC 10008: The new HTTP Query Method
blurb: The IETF published RFC 10008, defining a new HTTP QUERY method for sending safe, idempotent requests with a body, filling the gap between GET and POST. Unlike GET, it carries query input as request content rather than in the URI, and unlike POST, it is explicitly safe and idempotent, so requests can be automatically retried or cached without risk of partial state changes.

title: MolmoMotion: Language-guided 3D motion forecasting
blurb: AI2 released MolmoMotion, a model that forecasts an object's 3D point trajectory from a video frame and a written action instruction.

title: Is Meta destroying its engineering organization?
blurb: Meta's engineering culture has unraveled in just weeks under AI-driven leadership pressure, according to a Pragmatic Engineer report from Gergely Orosz. The piece details engineers being pushed to use AI tools at all times, core teams feeling sidelined, and a major outage, arguing leadership has recast software engineering from a profit center into a disdained cost center after two decades of "move fast" success.

title: Cybercriminals allegedly hacked tens of thousands of Fortinet firewalls used by major companies all over the world
blurb: Two cybersecurity firms reported that hackers compromised tens of thousands of Fortinet firewalls and VPNs at major companies including Accenture, Oracle, Samsung, and Siemens, in an ongoing campaign dubbed FortiBleed. The attackers exploited no zero-day, instead scanning for exposed devices and breaking in with previously leaked passwords, then using each compromised firewall to harvest more credentials and spread further. Fortinet said the data stems from prior incidents and credential bruteforcing.

title: How we run Firecracker VMs inside EC2 and start browsers in less than 1s
blurb: Browser Use rebuilt its cloud browser infrastructure on Firecracker microVMs running inside EC2, cutting session cost from $0.06 to $0.02 per browser hour and starting new browsers in under a second. Each session gets its own VM for isolation, replacing a Unikraft unikernel setup that handled idle shutdown well but couldn't autoscale fast enough during traffic spikes.

title: Databricks sales growth tops 80%, but margin are shrinking from swarm of AI agents
blurb: Databricks said annualized revenue jumped more than 80% from a year earlier to $6.9 billion, but CEO Ali Ghodsi told CNBC that gross margins are falling as AI agents generate far more queries and push up model costs. The consumption-based model now draws $1.7 billion a year from AI products, and at a $134 billion valuation Databricks is worth more than public rival Snowflake while staying off the IPO line.

title: Intel begins production of most-advanced chip, inching closer to possible Apple deal
blurb: Intel began risk production of 18A-P, its most advanced chip node, which delivers 9% higher performance or 18% lower power than the 18A process it already runs at volume in Arizona. The milestone moves Intel closer to a possible deal to manufacture chips for Apple, though analysts say outside customers will hinge on Intel hitting yield rates above 90% in the first month.

title: Apple is about to make Hide My Email useless
blurb: Apple is moving all Sign in with Apple and Hide My Email aliases to a dedicated @private.icloud.com subdomain, making it trivial for services to identify and block relay addresses without affecting regular iCloud mailboxes. The change preserves the carve-out that gave the feature its value, since aliases were previously hard to ban without hitting legitimate iCloud users, and could push many sites to reject them outright like disposable email.

title: A robot is sprinting towards you. Do you want it running on Claude or Grok?
blurb: OpenRouter's developer relations lead dropped 11 LLMs into a 2D battle royale and ran 30 games, with Grok 4.1 Fast winning 13 at $0.97 per win versus Claude Sonnet 4.6's 5 wins at $26.78 each. The model with the most kills, GPT 5.4, placed second on wins, and three models won zero games, suggesting standard benchmarks poorly predict agentic performance.

title: Z.ai pitches GLM-5.2 for long-running software engineering tasks
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an MIT-licensed open-source coding model with a one million-token context window, claiming it trails Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.8 by 1% on the FrontierSWE long-horizon benchmark and edges out OpenAI's GPT-5.5 by the same margin. The model uses a technique called IndexShare to cut per-token compute by 2.9x at full context, targeting the cost of running coding agents across large repositories.

title: Sweeping Credential-Harvesting Heist Compromises 30K+ Fortinet Devices
blurb: A Russian-speaking threat group has compromised more than 30,000 internet-facing Fortinet firewalls and VPN gateways across 194 countries, according to SOCRadar researchers who found the attackers' exposed server. The campaign relied on credential harvesting rather than a Fortinet flaw, targeting default, built-in, and long-lived accounts across telecom, government, healthcare, and financial sectors, with India and the US accounting for nearly a third of confirmed compromises.

title: Introducing LifeSciBench
blurb: OpenAI released LifeSciBench, an expert-written benchmark of 750 tasks that tests whether AI systems can handle real life science research rather than isolated biology questions. Built with 173 PhD-trained scientist contributors across seven workflows and seven biological domains, it grades free-response answers against 19,020 rubric criteria covering evidence handling, experiment design, translation, and scientific communication.

title: Coinbase Teases 1:1-Backed Tokenized U.S. Stocks With On-Chain Dividends
blurb: Coinbase said it will launch tokenized U.S. stocks backed one-for-one by real shares, with on-chain ownership, trading, redemption, and automatic dividends, though only in eligible jurisdictions outside the United States. The "not a derivative, not an IOU" framing targets market leader xStocks and rivals Robinhood, Gemini, and Kraken, but Coinbase named no custody partner, token issuer, supported equities, or launch date.

title: SubQ 1.1 Small
blurb: SubQ released the model card for SubQ 1.1 Small, a Subquadratic Sparse Attention model that hits near-perfect needle-in-a-haystack retrieval up to 12M tokens while cutting attention compute by nearly 1,000x. At 1M tokens it needs 64.5x less compute than dense attention and runs 56x faster than FlashAttention-2, with a broader lineup spanning 2M to 12M tokens planned for later this year.

title: Making ast.walk 220x Faster
blurb: Reflex rebuilt Python's `ast.walk` to run roughly 220x faster after the standard library version bottlenecked their AI code generator's custom linter. The speedup came from dropping generators and `yield` in the hot path, inlining `iter_child_nodes`, and replacing repeated suspend-resume overhead with a plain list that accumulates nodes during traversal.

title: Building AI Agents for AR Glasses and XR Devices with NVIDIA XR AI
blurb: NVIDIA released XR AI in public beta, an open-source library for building AI agents that run on AR glasses and XR headsets by routing live camera and microphone streams to GPU-accelerated cloud or edge models. The agents combine visual grounding via Cosmos, voice interaction through Nemotron, and enterprise tool use over MCP, with early deployments at Siemens factories and Stanford and Princeton research labs.

title: AI coding agents taught robots how to install GPUs and cut zip ties
blurb: Researchers at Nvidia's GEAR lab, Carnegie Mellon, and UC Berkeley built ENPIRE, an agent harness that lets AI coding agents autonomously train robots to cut zip ties and insert GPUs into tight motherboard sockets. Tested with OpenAI Codex, Claude Code, and Moonshot's Kimi Code, the system runs robots in parallel overnight, analyzes failure logs, and refines its own training algorithms, with the team planning to open-source everything.

title: AI and brain-computer interface allow speechless ALS patient to work a full-time job
blurb: A UC Davis research team paired a brain-computer interface with machine learning to let a speechless ALS patient hold down a full-time job by decoding his intended speech in real time. The implant hardware itself is not new, but the team's decoding software translates neural signals into synthesized words fast enough for live conversation and work, restoring practical communication that the disease had taken away.

title: Top announcements of the AWS Summit in New York, 2026
blurb: At its New York Summit, AWS introduced a wave of Amazon Bedrock AgentCore updates, including a managed Knowledge Base for enterprise RAG, a fully managed web search tool, and the now-generally-available AgentCore harness for building production agents from configuration. AWS WAF also added a capability that lets publishers price, meter, and charge AI bots for access to their content and APIs.

title: AI Startup Midjourney Pivots to Health With Ultrasound Machine
blurb: Midjourney announced its first hardware product, an ultrasound machine, marking an unexpected pivot from AI image generation into personal health and medical devices. The company revealed the project at a San Francisco event, signaling ambitions beyond the consumer image tools that built its reputation as it enters a heavily regulated industry dominated by established medical-imaging firms.

title: Astrobotic unveils Griffin-1 lunar lander
blurb: Astrobotic revealed Griffin-1, its second lunar lander, which it plans to launch in Q4 on a SpaceX Falcon Heavy after Peregrine failed to attempt a landing in 2024. Billed as the first infrastructure-class lunar lander, Griffin will carry 10 payloads from six nations, led by Astrolab's 500-kilogram FLEX rover, the heaviest commercial payload sent to the moon to date.

title: Intel’s performance-enhanced 18A-P process enters risk production — drop-in 18A upgrade promises 9% performance improvement at iso-power, cuts thermal resistance by 40%
blurb: Intel's 18A-P process, a performance-enhanced revision of its 18A node, has entered risk production, the low-volume stage before mass manufacturing. The node promises a 9% performance gain at the same power or an 18% power reduction at the same performance, plus 40% lower thermal resistance, and stays backward compatible so designers can port existing 18A chips like Panther Lake and Xeon 6+ without changes.

title: GLM-5.2: Built for Long-Horizon Tasks
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an MIT-licensed open model built for long-horizon coding agents with a 1M-token context and selectable thinking-effort levels. It ranks as the top open-source model on the FrontierSWE, PostTrainBench, and SWE-Marathon long-horizon benchmarks, trailing Anthropic's Opus 4.8 while edging out GPT-5.5 on FrontierSWE.

title: Brit competition cops order Google to make search rankings less mysterious
blurb: The UK's Competition and Markets Authority ordered Google to make its search ranking practices more transparent, requiring clearer disclosure of how results and prominence are determined. The order stems from Google's new "strategic market status" designation under the UK's digital markets regime, giving regulators standing power to impose conduct requirements on the company's search business.

title: Anthropic got hit by export rules nobody understands
blurb: The Trump administration ordered Anthropic to cut off all foreign nationals, including US-based users and its own employees, citing national security authorities under an export control directive that forced the company to block Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for everyone. Legal experts call the move unprecedented, since the models stay hosted on Anthropic's servers and users receive only chatbot responses, not weights or source code, leaving unclear what is actually being "exported.

title: Sooner than expected? Useful quantum error correction promised for 2028.
blurb: Amazon and QuEra promised useful, error-corrected quantum computing as soon as 2028, well ahead of the field's common five- to 10-year estimate. The claim hinges on scaling to thousands of high-quality hardware qubits to build the logical qubits error correction requires, where current technologies still trade qubit count against qubit quality.

title: Getting more from each token: How Copilot improves context handling and model routing
blurb: GitHub detailed how Copilot stretches each token through prompt caching and on-demand tool search, plus expanding Auto to pick the model per task.

title: Midjourney Medical
blurb: Midjourney, the AI image-generation startup, launched Midjourney Medical, a new effort applying its image models to healthcare and clinical imaging. The move marks the company's first expansion beyond consumer creative tools into a regulated vertical, where accuracy and safety requirements are far steeper than in general-purpose image generation.

title: Midjourney goes from generating cat images to full-body ultrasound scans
blurb: Midjourney revealed its first hardware product, The Midjourney Scanner, a full-body ultrasound system built with Butterfly Network that takes a 60-second scan and targets image quality comparable to MRI. The image-generation startup plans to deliver the scans through a San Francisco "spa" experience, with CEO David Holz pitching preventative full-body imaging frequent enough to run daily.

title: Nvidia reveals AI robots that taught themselves to install GPUs into motherboards — video shows robot ‘solve high-precision tasks like… installing GPUs all by itself’
blurb: Nvidia showcased ENPIRE, an open-source framework that gave eight Codex agents a fleet of robots, GPUs, and a token budget, then let them teach themselves high-precision tasks like slotting a graphics card into a motherboard, sorting metal pins, and cutting zip ties. The robots practiced on real hardware, read papers, and refined their own control code to fix failures, demonstrating autonomous research in the physical world.

title: Sources: ByteDance has been Microsoft's biggest AI customer in recent years, largely using OpenAI models, and is on track to spend $1B+ a year on Azure services (Bloomberg)
blurb: ByteDance has been Microsoft's largest AI customer in recent years, running mostly on OpenAI models and on track to spend more than $1B a year on Azure, according to Bloomberg. The relationship underscores how the TikTok parent leans on US cloud and model providers even as Washington scrutinizes its operations and pushes for a divestiture.

title: Nvidia researchers unveil ENPIRE, an agent harness framework that develops robotic self-improvement strategies for physical tasks with minimal human supervision (Jeremy Hsu/Ars Technica)
blurb: Nvidia researchers introduced ENPIRE, an agent harness that lets robots develop their own self-improvement strategies for physical tasks with minimal human supervision. The framework generates and refines its own training routines rather than relying on hand-tuned reward functions, aiming to cut the manual engineering that currently bottlenecks how quickly robots learn new real-world skills.

title: PsiQuantum breaks ground in Australia on what it says will be the world’s first utility-scale quantum computer
blurb: PsiQuantum broke ground on a Moreton Bay, Queensland facility where it plans to build what it calls the world's first utility-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computer, eventually housing tens of thousands of photonic quantum chips. The project is backed by nearly $1B in Australian and US government funding, though no company has yet demonstrated a fault-tolerant quantum computer at commercial scale.

title: Nvidia PCs don’t need cloud for AI
blurb: Nvidia announced RTX Spark at Computex 2026, a platform for slim Windows laptops and compact desktops that combines an Arm-based CPU, Blackwell RTX graphics, and a large unified memory architecture into a single AI-first system. The pitch is local-first AI, running the model, agent, data, and application on the machine itself rather than routing requests to a cloud service like ChatGPT or Gemini.

title: Apple showcases its new developer AI tools in impressive 90-minute presentation
blurb: Apple released a 90-minute WWDC26 developer session showing off Xcode 27, the Foundation Models framework, and new Siri AI integration, including a 20-minute demo that builds a full app with 3D animations and Visual Intelligence from a handful of prompts. The session closes by running the 1-trillion-parameter Kimi 2.6 model locally across four Mac Studios linked over RDMA-over-Thunderbolt.

title: Among the large new rockets Amazon was counting on, only Europe has delivered
blurb: Amazon has hundreds of completed Leo internet satellites sitting idle in Florida, with only Arianespace's Ariane 6 among its three new contracted rockets actually flying them to orbit. Blue Origin's New Glenn and ULA's Vulcan have yet to launch a single Leo satellite, leaving Amazon dependent on the soon-to-retire Atlas V and a French provider to build out its low-Earth-orbit constellation.

title: US holds off blacklisting DeepSeek, more than 100 firms deemed security risks
blurb: The US declined to add Chinese AI lab DeepSeek to its trade blacklist, even as it designated more than 100 other firms as national security risks. The decision spares DeepSeek the export restrictions and supply-chain cutoffs imposed on the newly listed companies, leaving the high-profile model maker in a regulatory gray zone amid escalating US-China tech tensions.

title: Helion secures world’s first regulatory licenses for fusion power plant being built in Washington
blurb: Helion Energy received the world's first regulatory licenses for a fusion power plant, granted by the Washington Department of Health for its Orion facility in Malaga. The 50-megawatt plant, slated to supply a Microsoft data center by 2028, reflects a 2023 NRC decision to regulate fusion like particle accelerators rather than fission reactors. Helion recently raised $463M, bringing total funding to $1.5B.

title: AMD acquires MEXT to add predictive memory optimization to its AI stack
blurb: AMD acquired memory optimization startup MEXT to fold predictive memory software into its AI infrastructure stack, with financial terms undisclosed. MEXT's technology uses AI to move frequently accessed data between flash and DRAM, extending effective memory capacity as enterprises grapple with DRAM prices Gartner expects to rise sharply through 2026.

title: World leaders want American AI. They just don’t want America to be able to turn it off.
blurb: At the G7 Summit, leaders including France's Emmanuel Macron and India's Narendra Modi warned that the US could revoke access to top American AI models at any time, citing the Trump administration's recent block on Anthropic exporting its Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models. In response, G7 members discussed a "trusted partners" scheme to grant non-US nations reliable access to advanced models from firms like Anthropic and OpenAI, bypassing unilateral US restrictions.

title: SpaceX leapfrogs Amazon and briefly tops Microsoft in market value on Cursor acquisition news
blurb: SpaceX briefly topped Microsoft and passed Amazon to become the world's fourth-most valuable public company, hitting a $2.94T market cap after announcing a $60B acquisition of AI coding startup Cursor. The deal, less than a week after SpaceX's $75B IPO, signals Musk's push to build a vertically integrated AI business spanning chips, data centers, software, satellite networks, and space infrastructure.

title: Nation-state hackers are increasingly using preinstalled software on low-cost home devices to create residential proxy networks and mask cyberattack traffic (Robert McMillan/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Nation-state hackers are increasingly hijacking preinstalled software on cheap home devices to build residential proxy networks that disguise cyberattack traffic as ordinary consumer browsing, according to a Wall Street Journal report. Because the malicious activity routes through legitimate home IP addresses, defenders struggle to distinguish it from real users, making the technique a growing blind spot for enterprise security teams.

title: First official details of AMD's next-gen 'Mustang Peak' Threadripper CPUs come into view — chips feature DDR5, PCIe 6.0, and a new socket
blurb: AMD's next-generation Threadripper CPUs, code-named Mustang Peak, will use Zen 6 cores built on a 2nm-class TSMC process, with DDR5 memory, PCIe 6.0, and a new TR6 platform. The leaked technical documentation points to up to 144 cores on Threadripper Pro parts, up from 96, with clock rates targeting well above 6 GHz.

title: Control whether your users can have temporary chats and delete conversations in the Gemini app
blurb: Google Workspace admins can now control whether users get temporary, unsaved Gemini chats and the ability to delete conversation history, on by default.

title: Microsoft Makes Big AI Inroads in China by Selling OpenAI Models
blurb: Microsoft has built a sizable business selling OpenAI models to Chinese companies, even as US-China tensions over AI escalate. The arrangement routes frontier models through Azure to customers in a market where domestic firms like Alibaba and DeepSeek dominate, giving Microsoft a foothold despite tightening export controls and political pressure on both sides.

title: AI demands more engineering discipline. Not less
blurb: The author argues that AI's ability to generate competent code raises the bar for engineering discipline rather than lowering it, drawing a parallel to the shift from hand-maintained servers to immutable infrastructure. Now that models like Opus 4.5 produce code on par with the median engineer, the human work moves toward stronger review, communication norms, and systems thinking rather than away from them.

title: NASA picks Eric Schmidt’s rocket company for Mars mission, setting up a race with SpaceX
blurb: NASA hired Relativity Space, the rocket maker Eric Schmidt acquired last year, to build and fly the Aeolus orbiter to Mars on a 2028 launch timeline. The mission's four instruments will deliver the first daily global view of Martian dust, winds, and temperature, using the same low-cost public-private contract model NASA pioneered with SpaceX for ISS cargo runs.

title: Chinese memory brands ditch Samsung and Micron for homegrown CXMT and YMTC silicon — Corsair, HP, and Dell are already adopting the China-produced DDR5 chips
blurb: Chinese memory brands are dropping Samsung, Micron, and SK Hynix in favor of homegrown DDR5 from CXMT and NAND from YMTC, both heavily subsidized by Beijing to prioritize domestic supply over data-center margins. The shift is already reaching Western vendors, with Corsair shipping CXMT chips in some Vengeance kits and HP and Dell qualifying the China-produced silicon for their own systems.

title: AI is hurting Apple in more ways than one: it may force iPhone price increases
blurb: Tim Cook told the WSJ that price increases on Apple devices are "unavoidable," blaming a global memory-chip shortage driven by AI demand that has quadrupled storage costs since last year. The next iPhone, due in September, is the likeliest to see a hike, with research firm TechInsights estimating Apple would need to add $270 to the iPhone Pro to hold its margin.

title: Apple collects every tap to deliver App Store personalized recommendations
blurb: Apple's new App Store Personalized Collections feature logs every tap users make to power its tailored app recommendations, and security researchers at Mysk found the analytics capture extends to identifiable data like typing speed during searches. There is no toggle to disable the tracking, which sits at odds with Apple's privacy-focused marketing and its usual practice of offering opt-out controls for data collection.

title: Lore – Open source version control system designed for scalability
blurb: Lore is an open source, centralized version control system from Epic Games built for scale, representing repository state as Merkle trees and an immutable revision chain. It is optimized for binary-first storage with deduplication and sparse, on-demand data hydration, targeting the large-asset repositories that strain Git.

title: Intel starts cooking up enhanced 18A-P silicon for would-be foundry customers
blurb: Intel began producing 18A-P, an enhanced version of its 18A process that adds higher performance and clock speeds, to court external foundry customers. The node is a refinement of the 18A technology already used in Intel's own Panther Lake chips, and serves as a bridge to the 14A process Intel is pitching to win contract manufacturing deals against TSMC and Samsung.

title: Apple confirms price increases are coming to its products due to RAM shortage
blurb: Apple CEO Tim Cook told The Wall Street Journal that price increases across the company's products are "unavoidable," citing a DRAM shortage driven by memory being diverted to high-bandwidth chips for AI servers. Cook declined to specify timing or which devices are affected, though Macs and iPads could rise first, following last month's Mac mini base-price bump.

title: SAGE: Stochastic Prompt Optimization via Agent-Guided Exploration
blurb: SAGE treats prompt optimization as black-box search, using a multi-agent pipeline with diagnostic code execution to compound noisy A/B tests into robust gains.

title: Volkswagen started blocking GrapheneOS users
blurb: Volkswagen's myVW app began blocking GrapheneOS users, the privacy-focused Android fork, by enforcing Google Play Services integrity checks that flag the de-Googled OS. A forum thread documents users restoring partial functionality through workarounds like temporarily enabling Play Services, highlighting the growing friction between hardened mobile operating systems and apps that rely on Google's attestation framework.

title: US pulls the 'kill-switch' on Anthropic's Fable 5 AI models, sending global allies scrambling — European and Canadian leaders alarm allies over sudden export bans
blurb: The Trump administration blocked exports of Anthropic's Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models, cutting off allied governments from direct access to the company's frontier systems. European and Canadian leaders warned that the sudden restrictions could force them to build their own national models, signaling that AI access is becoming a lever of geopolitical leverage rather than a settled commercial market.

title: After unveiling ridiculously expensive AR glasses, Snap’s stock takes a dive
blurb: Snap launched Specs, its long-developed AR glasses, at a retail price of nearly $2,200, and the stock fell more than 5% the next day to a low of $4.83. CEO Evan Spiegel defended the price by framing Specs as a high-end computer positioned between Meta's cheaper Ray-Bans and Apple's bulkier Vision Pro, but the cost sits far above what Snap's teenage core users typically spend.

title: World model maker Odyssey nabs $1.45B valuation backed by Amazon and other big names
blurb: Odyssey raised a $310M Series B at a $1.45B valuation, led by Natural Capital with Amazon, AMD Ventures, and GV participating. The startup, founded by self-driving veterans Oliver Cameron and Jeff Hawke, builds world models that simulate physical environments with accurate physics, and as part of the deal will make AWS its preferred cloud and optimize its models for Amazon's Trainium chips.

title: Google bets on Gemini to reinvent the smart home speaker
blurb: Google introduced the $99.99 Google Home Speaker, its first standalone smart speaker since 2020's Nest Audio and the first built specifically for Gemini, with support for natural-language multistep commands, mid-sentence corrections, and 10 conversational voices. More advanced features sit behind a Google Home Premium subscription at $10 per month or $100 per year, including free-flowing Gemini Live conversations.

title: Midjourney unveils its first hardware product, the Midjourney Scanner, an ultrasound-based full-body scanner; it is unclear how AI fits into the medical effort (Richard Lawler/The Verge)
blurb: Midjourney introduced its first hardware product, the Midjourney Scanner, an ultrasound-based full-body scanner that marks the AI image lab's move into medical devices. The company has not explained how its image-generation models factor into the effort, leaving the device's connection to Midjourney's core AI work unclear.

title: A survey of US adults: 49% reported using chatbots in 2026, up from 33% in 2024, 24% said that they use chatbots on a daily basis, and 44% said they use ChatGPT (Pew Research Center)
blurb: Pew Research found that 49% of US adults reported using AI chatbots in 2026, up from 33% in 2024, with 24% now using them daily. ChatGPT remains dominant at 44% of adults, indicating that conversational AI has crossed into mainstream consumer habit rather than staying confined to early adopters.

title: Star Google Researcher Joins OpenAI in Coup for ChatGPT Creator
blurb: A prominent Google AI researcher is leaving Alphabet for OpenAI, according to Bloomberg, a notable defection in the contest to build the most capable AI models. The move adds to the talent pressure on Google as rival labs compete for a small pool of senior researchers central to frontier model development.

title: Apple to Raise Prices Due to Memory Chip Crunch, Tim Cook Says
blurb: Apple will raise prices on some products as a memory chip shortage drives component costs higher, CEO Tim Cook told the Wall Street Journal, calling the increases "unavoidable." Surging demand for memory from AI data centers has tightened supply and lifted prices across the industry, squeezing hardware makers that compete for the same chips.

title: SMI says Nvidia is driving its consumer PCIe 6.0 roadmap, not AMD and Intel — RTX Spark agentic AI platform fuels a hunger for storage bandwidth
blurb: Silicon Motion will align its consumer PCIe 6.0 SSD controller roadmap with Nvidia rather than AMD or Intel, with a client-grade Gen6 platform targeted for late 2027. The company credits Nvidia's data-hungry client chips like GB10 and future RTX Spark for driving the timeline, since AMD and Intel are in no rush to adopt the costly interface and only enterprise Gen6 drives ship this year.

title: Amazon has lagged OpenAI and Anthropic, but AI chief sees path to catch up in 'coming year'
blurb: Amazon's AI chief Peter DeSantis told CNBC the company aims to compete with OpenAI and Anthropic on frontier models within the "coming year," acknowledging its Nova2 model isn't yet at the cutting edge. Amazon is pairing its Bedrock model marketplace with in-house Trainium and Graviton chips, and CEO Andy Jassy has floated selling Trainium racks to third parties to challenge Nvidia.

title: Microsoft launches Copilot Cowork globally for Microsoft 365
blurb: Microsoft made Copilot Cowork generally available worldwide for Microsoft 365 Copilot customers, following a three-month preview adopted by over half the Fortune 500. The cloud-hosted agentic system runs long, multi-tool tasks end-to-end to deliver finished outputs, billed per usage in Copilot Credits, with model choice spanning Anthropic Opus 4.8, Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.5, and an upcoming lower-cost Cowork 1.

title: Claude Design will now stick to your brand guidelines instead of generic AI mockups
blurb: Anthropic updated Claude Design, its AI visual creation tool, to build against imported brand design systems rather than generic mockups, pulling approved components from GitHub repos, design files, or uploaded assets and checking output against them before display. The release also adds a `/design-sync` handoff to Claude Code, a rebuilt drag-and-resize editor, PDF and PowerPoint export, and integrations with Adobe, Canva, Figma-adjacent tools, Vercel, and others.

title: Intel's fab roadmap examined — Arizona, Ohio, Ireland, and the two deadlines deciding 14A process node
blurb: Intel reversed course in a year, scrapping planned fabs in Germany and Poland and buying back its Ireland fab stake from Apollo for $14.2B before declaring "unprecedented demand for silicon." The next wave of capacity now hinges on two 2026-2027 deadlines: when 14A customers commit to firm orders, and a 35% US manufacturing tax credit that only applies to construction breaking ground before December 31st, 2026.

title: [AINews] Midjourney Medical: scan your organs like you step on a scale
blurb: Midjourney announced the Midjourney Scanner, a full-body ultrasonic CT system that founder David Holz called the first new whole-body imaging modality in 50 years. The Gen 1 prototype uses 358,000 ultrasonic elements across a 70cm ring to capture body slices at 0.5mm resolution without radiation, marking the bootstrapped image-generation lab's first move into medical hardware.

title: Estonia intends to recognize AI agents with digital IDs
blurb: Estonia plans to issue digital IDs to AI agents, extending its e-identity system to recognize autonomous software as distinct, verifiable actors. The move would give agents authenticated credentials for transactions and signatures, an early government attempt to build identity and accountability infrastructure for the agentic AI era.

title: VSCO launches Studio Pro mobile photo editing app and plans $500 per year subscription
blurb: VSCO launched Studio Pro, a mobile photo editing app on iOS with macOS support coming later this year, offering batch editing, reference-image style matching, and delivery through VSCO Galleries. The app anchors a new $500-per-year VSCO One bundle aimed at professional photographers, priced on par with Adobe Creative Cloud Pro and packaging editing, client delivery, and business tools into one subscription.

title: SpaceX's all-stock Cursor deal illustrates the power of public markets, which could help SpaceX narrow the gap with Anthropic and OpenAI through acquisitions (New York Times)
blurb: SpaceX acquired Cursor in an all-stock deal, a transaction the New York Times frames as a demonstration of how access to liquid equity can fund aggressive expansion. The piece argues that SpaceX's high-value stock gives it acquisition firepower to close the gap with AI leaders like Anthropic and OpenAI without spending cash.

title: Anthropic asked for regulation. Washington went much further
blurb: The Trump administration ordered Anthropic to suspend access to its newest Claude models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, for any foreign national, citing unspecified national security authorities. The directive lands days after CEO Dario Amodei called for binding AI regulation that lets the government block unsafe models, and Anthropic, now valued near $1 trillion, disputes the recall as a "misunderstanding" it is racing to resolve.

title: Sources: JPMorgan Chase has stopped its staff in Hong Kong from accessing Anthropic's AI models, following a similar move by rival Goldman Sachs (Financial Times)
blurb: JPMorgan Chase has barred its Hong Kong staff from accessing Anthropic's AI models, following a similar restriction by rival Goldman Sachs. The moves point to growing caution among major banks over third-party AI tools in sensitive markets, where data security and regulatory exposure outweigh the productivity gains from frontier models.

title: A Blackstone-led consortium agrees to take control of software company Medallia from Thoma Bravo, which will lose the entire $5B it invested in Medallia (Financial Times)
blurb: A Blackstone-led consortium agreed to take control of customer-experience software maker Medallia from Thoma Bravo, wiping out the entire $5B Thoma Bravo invested in the company. The deal marks a rare total loss for the private equity firm, which took Medallia private in 2021 and now exits without recovering any of its equity.

title: Apple to Launch Camera Airpods, Foldable iPhone in 2027
blurb: Apple plans to launch camera-equipped AirPods, smart glasses, and its first foldable iPhone in 2027 as part of a two-year hardware push, according to Bloomberg's Mark Gurman. The roadmap pits Apple against Snap, which just introduced its Specs augmented-reality glasses, as both companies race to define the wearable computing form factor beyond the smartphone.

title: CEOs of Anthropic and Google DeepMind call for U.S.-led AI coalition in meeting at G7
blurb: Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei and Google DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis called for a U.S.-led international coalition to set AI rules and standards at a closed-door G7 lunch with President Trump and roughly a dozen tech executives. Amodei proposed structured access to frontier models and a chip trade bloc that excludes China, days after U.S. export controls forced Anthropic to disable its newest Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models.

title: Epic Games just took a big step toward AI-built games with Unreal Engine 5.8
blurb: Epic Games released Unreal Engine 5.8, the final major UE5 release, with an experimental Model Context Protocol plugin that connects any LLM to core engine systems like blueprints, assets, and materials. Epic demonstrated Anthropic's Claude Code arranging scene objects and adjusting lighting, and framed the plugin as groundwork for deeper LLM integration in Unreal Engine 6, targeting early access in late 2027.

title: Midjourney, the AI image generator, is developing a full-body ultrasonic scanner
blurb: Midjourney, the AI image generator, is building a full-body ultrasonic scanner that maps the body to a fraction of a millimeter in under 60 seconds, roughly 100 times faster than a full-body MRI. The company is developing the hardware with Butterfly Network under an exclusive license to its ultrasound-on-chip technology, and plans to house the machines in dedicated spas under a new Midjourney Medical division.

title: DeepSeek was set to be added to US Entity List for supporting China’s military and intelligence operations, report claims — White House holds off to avoid escalating tensions with China
blurb: The Trump administration drafted plans to add DeepSeek and memory maker CXMT to the US Entity List over their alleged support for China's military and intelligence operations, but the White House has held off on the designation, according to a report. The delay reflects an effort to avoid escalating trade tensions with Beijing, leaving both companies able to source US technology for now.

title: Understanding the "Airport" Censorship Circumvention Ecosystem in China
blurb: A new study presents the first systematic look at "airports," subscription-based proxy services that over half of 1,667 surveyed Chinese users rely on to bypass the Great Firewall and reach services like ChatGPT and Netflix. Researchers identified 3,431 active airports built on a few open-source toolkits, finding their multi-hop architecture often outperforms direct connections but introduces new risks from payment tracking, government takedowns, and operators' own censorship policies.

title: Clojure Hosted on Go
blurb: Glojure is a Go-hosted Clojure interpreter that exposes Go libraries to Clojure the way the JVM exposes Java, currently at v0.3.0.

title: ChatGPT's image generator can be manipulated to produce violent, sexual content
blurb: Mindgard research found that ChatGPT's image generator can be manipulated into producing violent and sexually explicit content, including depictions of death and sexual violence, without users directly requesting it. The findings follow earlier reports of the tool generating nude images that OpenAI said it had fixed, and renew questions about why these models are trained on such material and why content filters keep failing.

title: SteamOS Linux 3.8 released as stable
blurb: Valve released SteamOS 3.8 as a stable build, the Arch Linux-based operating system that powers the Steam Deck and its gaming-focused desktop and handheld mode. The release continues Valve's push to extend SteamOS beyond its own hardware to third-party handhelds, strengthening Linux as a viable PC gaming platform against Windows.

title: Only 16 percent of Americans think AI will have a positive impact on society, a new study shows
blurb: A new Pew Research study found that only 16% of Americans believe AI will have a positive impact on society over the next 20 years, while roughly 40% expect a negative one and nearly two-thirds say development is moving too fast. The pessimism persists even as adoption climbs, with about a quarter of Americans using chatbots daily and 44% now reporting ChatGPT use, more than double the 2023 figure.

title: Solid-State Batteries Take to the Sky
blurb: A team flew a modified motorized glider on solid-state batteries, reaching 410 Wh/kg versus 250 Wh/kg for a standard lithium-ion pack and climbing to 24,000 feet with help from wing-mounted solar panels. The gain comes from a solid lithium anode, which stores more energy than graphite and resists thermal runaway, though scaling cheap manufacturing remains the barrier to electric aircraft.

title: 'The retail SSD market has almost disappeared,' says Silicon Motion exec — PC OEMs are buying third-party drives as direct NAND supply dries up
blurb: The retail SSD market has nearly vanished in the first half of 2026, a Silicon Motion VP told Tom's Hardware, as NAND makers redirect supply from consumer drives to AI data centers. PC OEMs like Dell, HP, and Acer can no longer source enough NAND directly, so they increasingly buy finished drives from module makers, leaving controller vendors like Silicon Motion and Phison as the rare winners.

title: The Trump Mobile T1 was supposed to be made in America. It’s a Taiwanese phone with a gold coat.
blurb: The Trump Mobile T1, pitched a year ago as a US-made gold iPhone rival, shipped as a rebranded HTC U24 Pro, a Taiwanese midrange Android from 2024 painted what Bloomberg's reviewer calls "more mustard yellow than gold." At $499 it runs a Snapdragon 7 Gen 3, ships with Truth Social preinstalled, and is stuck on a February 2026 security patch with no clear update schedule.

title: Relativity Space to privately develop Mars orbiter mission
blurb: Relativity Space announced its Interplanetary Sciences Program, with a first mission to launch a Mars science and telecommunications orbiter in 2028 aboard its Terran R reusable rocket. The privately developed spacecraft will carry a NASA Ames atmospheric instrument suite called Aeolus and a subsurface-ice radar, while doubling as a laser and radio relay node for craft on the Martian surface.

title: Uncle Sam bets $500M that Alphabet spinoff's AI can dig up new semiconductor materials
blurb: The US government committed up to $500M to back SandboxAQ, the AI startup spun out of Alphabet, to use its models to discover new semiconductor materials. The bet ties federal funding to AI-driven materials research, aiming to speed up the search for chip compounds that could ease supply-chain bottlenecks and keep domestic semiconductor development competitive.

title: Tim Cook says RAM expenses are ‘unsustainable’ and Apple is going to raise prices
blurb: Tim Cook told the Wall Street Journal that surging memory costs have become "unsustainable" and that price increases across the iPhone, iPad, and Mac are unavoidable. Apple has already dropped its cheapest Mac Mini and Mac Studio configurations, and the WSJ estimates the upcoming iPhone 18 Pro could start at $1,299, up from $1,099, as AI data center demand drives a broad RAM and storage shortage.

title: China pushes for AI safety as G7 summit wraps up without Beijing
blurb: Chinese officials said Beijing is accelerating plans for a global AI cooperation organization and pushing cheap, openly downloadable models to the Global South, just as the G7 discussed restricting top US models to "trusted partners." The split reflects two competing strategies: the US gating subscription-only frontier models behind alliances, while China courts developing economies through BRICS, the UN, and free access.

title: OpenAI readies ChatGPT for Science subscription plan
blurb: OpenAI is preparing a dedicated "ChatGPT for Science" subscription aimed at universities, national laboratories, and corporate R&D groups working in biology, chemistry, physics, and materials. The plan extends OpenAI's vertical strategy into research and would compete directly with Anthropic's Claude for Life Sciences and Google's Gemini-based co-scientist, though pricing, eligibility, and a launch timeline remain unconfirmed.

title: Ineffable Intelligence and Google Cloud partner on new frontier AI lab
blurb: Ineffable Intelligence selected Google Cloud as its preferred cloud partner to supply the infrastructure for its new London-based frontier AI lab. The deal follows Ineffable's $1.1B seed round, the largest in European history, and centers on reinforcement learning systems that generate and learn from real-time experience rather than static datasets, demanding tightly integrated training and inference at scale.

title: The IPO Onslaught Is Forcing Bankers to Pick Teams
blurb: Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley are forming separate internal teams to handle the expected IPOs of OpenAI and Anthropic, walling off bankers to prevent information sharing between the rival accounts. The arrangement reflects how the AI listing pipeline is straining Wall Street, with the two banks racing to win mandates on what could be among the largest tech offerings in years.

title: Leak confirms OpenAI is testing a ChatGPT for Science subscription
blurb: A leaked reference on ChatGPT's web build points to "ChatGPT for Science," a new subscription OpenAI is testing for scientific research use cases. The tier would likely restrict access to verified universities and institutions, extending the specialized grounding behind OpenAI's enterprise-locked GPT-Rosalind model to a broader set of research organizations rather than select partners.

title: Odyssey raises $310M at $1.45B valuation to transform AI model simulation
blurb: Odyssey, an AI lab building world models, raised $310M in Series B funding at a $1.45B valuation, led by Natural Capital with Amazon, AMD Ventures, GV, and EQT joining. The round came alongside an AWS deal giving Odyssey preferred access to Amazon's Trainium silicon to train world models, AI systems that simulate physics and causality for robotics and autonomous vehicles.

title: Only half of US datacenter capacity planned for 2026 is actually  under construction
blurb: Only about half of the US datacenter capacity slated to come online in 2026 is currently under construction, according to a new analysis, suggesting the AI-driven buildout is running well behind its announced targets. The gap points to bottlenecks in power, permitting, and supply chains rather than demand, leaving operators at risk of missing capacity commitments even as hyperscalers keep pledging billions for new sites.

title: California says AT&T lied to FCC in attempt to shut off old phone network
blurb: California regulators told the FCC that AT&T lied in its bid to shut off copper phone service for about 199,000 customers, disputing the company's claim that state rules force it to maintain aging copper lines. The June 15 filing says the CPUC has long allowed carriers to replace copper with fiber, and follows AT&T's lawsuit against California and its petition asking the FCC to override state service rules.

title: FTC lawsuit reveals how subscription scam networks evade app store enforcement
blurb: The FTC sued Genesis Tech, alleging its network of Cyprus-registered shell companies behind apps like MadMuscles, Nebula, and PDF Guru ran auto-renewing subscription scams that evaded Apple and Google fraud monitoring for years. By continually spinning up new corporate entities and merchant accounts, the network booked nearly a quarter-billion dollars in revenue while making subscriptions easy to start and nearly impossible to cancel.

title: ASML CEO Sees Musk’s Terafab as Test for Supply Lines
blurb: ASML CEO Christophe Fouquet said the chipmaking equipment supplier must guard against supply constraints as it services massive new projects like Elon Musk's Terafab. The comment frames Terafab's scale as a stress test for the lithography supply chain, where ASML's monopoly on EUV machines makes its capacity a hard ceiling on how fast new fabs can come online.

title: South Korean and Taiwanese tech companies that helped build China's hardware sector during the smartphone boom now reap the AI boom as US curbs sideline China (New York Times)
blurb: South Korean and Taiwanese suppliers that built China's hardware base during the smartphone era are now the biggest winners of the AI boom, according to the New York Times, as US export curbs steer chip and component demand away from Chinese firms. Memory makers, packaging specialists, and contract manufacturers in both countries are absorbing orders for AI servers and accelerators that might otherwise have gone to mainland competitors.

title: Epic wants to let you bring your Fortnite skins to other games
blurb: Epic Games will use Unreal Engine 6 to let developers build games that accept players' Fortnite skins and create their own skins that work in Fortnite, its first concrete step toward an interoperable metaverse. Unreal Engine 6 hits early access in late 2027 with a full release 12 to 18 months later, and adoption depends on developers building the support, which Epic has not yet incentivized.

title: Apple Prepares Second-Generation iPhone Air for Spring 2027
blurb: Apple is preparing a second-generation iPhone Air for spring 2027, aiming to boost the appeal of the ultra-thin device after a muted reception for the first model. The follow-up signals Apple is committed to the slimmed-down line rather than retiring it, betting that design refinements can win over buyers who balked at the original's tradeoffs.

title: Amazon AI exec predicts first 'commercially useful' quantum computers in 5-7 years
blurb: Amazon AI chief Peter DeSantis told CNBC that the first "commercially useful" small-scale quantum computers will arrive within five to seven years, the company's first public timeline for the technology. DeSantis expects quantum systems to then scale on a Moore's Law-like curve, a forecast that sits between Google's roughly five-year estimate and Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang's view that 15 years would be early.

title: SpaceX adds longtime Elon Musk ally Roelof Botha to board
blurb: SpaceX named longtime Elon Musk ally Roelof Botha an independent director and audit committee member, expanding its board to eight seats less than a week after the company's record Nasdaq IPO. Botha, a former Sequoia managing director who Musk recruited to PayPal in 2000, joins a board with limited oversight power, since Musk controls over 82% of SpaceX voting rights.

title: Robotic teleoperation data startup XDOF launches with $70M in funding
blurb: XDOF launched with $70M from Thrive Capital, Spark Capital, Andreessen Horowitz, Lux Capital, and WndrCo to build data pipelines and annotation tools for training robots on real-world physical tasks. The startup also released ABC-130K, which it calls the largest open-source bimanual robot manipulation dataset, betting that scarce teleoperation data, not models or chips, is the main bottleneck in physical AI.

title: Matter 1.6 and Product Security 1.1 officially announced, here’s what’s new
blurb: The Connectivity Standards Alliance announced Matter 1.6, a feature release that lets the full device commissioning exchange run over NFC so a smart bulb or in-wall switch can be set up before it has power. The update also adds Joint Fabric for shared multi-ecosystem network administration and standardized thermostat suggestions, alongside a companion Product Security 1.1 specification.

title: U.S. gov't asks court to dismiss NAACP lawsuit against Elon Musk's xAI over use of unpermitted gas turbines — DOJ says Grok model running at Colossus 2 ‘supports mission-critical operations’
blurb: The DOJ filed a memorandum backing the dismissal of an NAACP lawsuit that targets xAI's use of unpermitted natural gas turbines at its Colossus 2 data center, arguing that cutting power would threaten national, economic, and energy security. The government said Grok is one of only four AI models cleared to support military operations on Secret and Top-Secret networks, elevating a local environmental permitting fight into a national security dispute.

title: AI material discovery startup CuspAI reportedly raising $400M round
blurb: CuspAI, a UK startup using AI to accelerate materials discovery, is reportedly raising $400M at a $2.6B valuation, with Bezos Expeditions and Kleiner Perkins among the backers. The round values the company more than five times higher than its last, as early customers including ASML, Meta, and Hyundai use its platform to generate and simulate new material designs.

title: Apple investors are running out of patience with its AI promises
blurb: Apple's stock had its worst week since February after WWDC failed to convince Wall Street that its repeatedly delayed AI-driven iPhone upgrade cycle is any closer. The overhauled Siri launches this autumn as a beta built on a custom Google Gemini model running on Nvidia GPUs, won't reach the EU or China initially, and prompted no analyst to revise 2027 or 2028 revenue estimates.

title: Siri AI will make the Apple Watch a fully-fledged AI wearable in watchOS 27
blurb: Apple will bring Siri AI to the Apple Watch in watchOS 27, adding a dedicated Siri app and extending Apple Intelligence to the wrist for the first time. The feature isn't in the first beta but arrives later in the cycle, positioning the Watch as Apple's always-on wearable AI device alongside iPhone and AirPods; watchOS 27 requires Series 9, Ultra 2, or SE 3 and later.

title: Former Palantir employees raise $60M for AI enterprise startup Conduct
blurb: Conduct, a London-based enterprise AI startup founded by three former Palantir employees, raised $60M in Series A funding co-led by Index Ventures and Iconiq, with a strategic investment from SAP. The startup analyzes code and configurations across legacy ERP systems, particularly SAP, to speed migrations and cut IT maintenance for customers including Daimler Truck, Heidelberg Materials, and DHL.

title: Mistral CEO pitches open source AI days after Anthropic ban: ‘We exist outside of state control’
blurb: Mistral CEO Arthur Mensch pitched the company's open-source models as a sovereign alternative, days after the US government ordered Anthropic to cut two top models off from foreign nationals. The Paris-based scaleup, which has raised €3.5B and is reportedly in talks for another €3B at a ~€20B valuation, is courting European customers spooked by reliance on US providers.

title: Vercel launches a new framework and enterprise controls for agentic AI infrastructure
blurb: Vercel introduced a suite of agentic AI infrastructure products at its Ship conference, including Vercel Services for single-commit full-stack deployment, the Agent Stack coding bundle, an open-source agent framework called eve, and enterprise controls like short-lived credential tokens via Vercel Connect. The company says coding agents now trigger more than half of all commits on its platform, up from under 3% at the start of the year.

title: After taking Nvidia’s money, Odyssey raises $310M and bets on Amazon and AMD instead
blurb: Odyssey raised $310M at a $1.45B valuation to build general-purpose world models, in a round led by Natural Capital with Amazon, AMD Ventures, GV, EQT, and In-Q-Tel participating. The deal names AWS as Odyssey's preferred cloud provider and supplies it with Amazon's Trainium chips, a pointed pivot four months after Nvidia's venture arm backed the lab's Series A.

title: Sixty percent of US consumers say 'AI' in brand messaging is a turnoff
blurb: A WordPress VIP survey found that 60% of US consumers say "AI" in brand messaging is a turnoff rather than a selling point, and 61% can't name a single company using AI well in its marketing. The report argues two years of spending on AI brand visibility has produced no winners, while 74% of consumers say the web feels less human than a decade ago and bot fatigue sets in after about 40 minutes.

title: Mastodon looks to newsletters to help revive the open social web
blurb: Mastodon added email newsletters in its 4.6 release, letting writers send posts directly to subscribers' inboxes without requiring a Mastodon account. The feature targets institutional users, journalists, and bloggers, and aims to grow the platform beyond its 735,000 monthly active users, down from a peak above 2 million, by tying portable audiences to email rather than another social signup.

title: Deutsches Roboter-Startup sammelt 1,4 Milliarden Dollar ein
blurb: Neura Robotics, a German robotics startup, raised up to $1.4B in a Series C round backed by Amazon, Nvidia, Qualcomm, Bosch, Schaeffler, Tether, and the European Investment Bank. The company says it is the largest funding round ever for a full-stack robotics firm and plans to use the capital to scale production of humanoid robots and its "physical AI" platform, targeting several million units by 2030.

title: Collecting robot training data is dirty, unglamorous work. Some AI labs are already paying XDOF to do it.
blurb: XDOF emerged from stealth with $70M from Thrive Capital, Spark Capital, a16z, Lux, and WndrCo to build the data pipelines, collection tools, and annotation systems that robotics labs struggle to assemble themselves. The 60-person startup says it already works with 20 customers, including several frontier AI labs, betting that the next AI bottleneck is physical-interaction training data rather than models or chips.

title: Two Stanford grads raise $11M to build a noninvasive wearable for hormone tracking
blurb: Clair Health, founded by Stanford grads Jenny Duan and Abhinav Agarwal, raised $11.6M to build a noninvasive wearable that tracks hormonal health markers like inflammation, energy, and cycle phase. The round was led by Khosla Ventures with participation from a16z speedrun and Anne Wojcicki, and the device pairs new sensors with voice-based onboarding the company says can classify a user's cycle phase after a few minutes of conversation.

title: As tech giants rush to build AI infrastructure, some residents living near data centers say a constant infrasonic vibration is ruining their health and homes (Adeel Hassan/New York Times)
blurb: Residents living near AI data centers told the New York Times that a constant low-frequency infrasonic hum is damaging their health and homes, with complaints of headaches, sleeplessness, and persistent vibration. The story surfaces a growing local cost of the AI infrastructure boom, as the power and cooling demands of hyperscale compute push industrial facilities closer to where people live.

title: Circles Sold Phone Spy Tools to Repressive States, Report Says
blurb: Circles, a Bulgaria-based company, sold phone surveillance technology to governments with histories of repression, letting authorities track mobile phones and intercept private communications, according to documents obtained by Human Rights Watch. The report adds to scrutiny of the commercial spyware industry, where firms operating from inside the EU continue supplying tracking and interception tools to states that use them against dissidents and journalists.

title: BWX Agrees to License Nuclear Reactor Design After Activist Push
blurb: BWX Technologies agreed to license its small modular reactor design, moving the nuclear company toward commercializing technology it had developed but not brought to market. The deal follows pressure from activist investor Ananym Capital Management, which urged BWX to monetize the design rather than keep it in-house.

title: Can Musk Pull Off a SpaceX-Tesla Merger?
blurb: Bloomberg Opinion columnist Liam Denning argues that an Elon Musk-led merger of SpaceX and Tesla would create serious conflicts of interest, even as the idea circulates. He notes the two companies have different investor bases, risk profiles, and capital needs, making a combination hard to structure cleanly despite Musk's history of moving assets between his ventures.

title: Allbirds continues AI pivot with name change and CEO hire, sending stock soaring
blurb: Allbirds is renaming itself Smartbird and named Nadia Carlsten, former head of AWS's quantum computing center and most recently CEO of AI infrastructure firm DCAI, as its new chief executive. Shares jumped 39% on the news, extending a rally that began with April's pivot from shoemaking to AI compute infrastructure, when the company rebranded to NewBird AI and saw its market cap rise sevenfold.

title: We Did the Math on Why the iPhone 18 Pro Could Cost $1,299
blurb: A WSJ analysis estimates the iPhone 18 Pro could reach $1,299 as a global memory-chip shortage drives up component costs across consumer electronics. Apple's scale and supply-chain leverage usually shield it from price swings, but tighter DRAM and NAND supply is squeezing margins enough that even Apple may pass the increase on to buyers.

title: DOJ Defends Musk’s xAI in Data-Center Pollution Lawsuit
blurb: The Justice Department filed in support of Elon Musk's xAI in a lawsuit over pollution from its Memphis-area data center in Mississippi, arguing the facility trains AI models the government relies on for national-security work. The intervention ties xAI's compute buildout to federal interests, complicating environmental challenges to the energy-hungry data centers powering frontier model training.

title: China conducts 4 launches in 3 days, but silence follows Kuaizhou–11 launch
blurb: China carried out four orbital launches in three days, including a Kuaizhou-11 solid rocket that fell silent for more than eight hours after liftoff from Jiuquan, a pattern that usually signals a launch failure or payload trouble. The successful missions added nine more Guowang broadband satellites via a Long March 12, bringing that 13,000-satellite constellation to 177 in orbit, and sent the experimental Shijian-31 toward geostationary orbit.

title: Trump Says Anthropic Negotiations Continue as AI Leaders Huddle at G-7
blurb: President Trump said negotiations with Anthropic are continuing as the company's AI models remain shut down, comments he made at a Group of Seven summit. Some world leaders at the gathering voiced concern about losing access to leading AI tools during the impasse, raising the stakes on a dispute that has moved from a corporate matter to one drawing attention from heads of state.

title: Google prepares Personalization and AI Editing for NotebookLM
blurb: Google is preparing two NotebookLM features, Personal Intelligence and AI Editing, both surfacing in testing ahead of a likely rollout. Personal Intelligence gives the tool memory of past conversations, limited to activity inside NotebookLM rather than Gmail or Docs, while AI Editing lets users push saved note text back into chat to rework it, closing a gap where responses couldn't be changed once created.

title: Snap Inc. launches new SPECS augmented reality glasses (for 'just' $2,195)
blurb: Snap Inc. released SPECS, standalone augmented reality glasses that run on two Snapdragon processors and start at $2,195. The tetherless design uses an LCOS display with a 51-degree field of view, delivers 7ms motion-to-photon latency, and runs about four hours on a charge or 20 hours of mixed use with the included case.

title: Apple’s new software drops support for these 16 products
blurb: While iOS 27 keeps every iPhone iOS 26 supported, macOS Golden Gate, iPadOS 27, watchOS 27, and tvOS 27 drop 16 devices total.

title: iOS 27 makes your iPhone faster in 40+ ways, here’s the full list
blurb: Apple detailed more than 40 performance improvements coming in iOS 27, spanning faster app launches, quicker system animations, and reduced load times across the OS. While Apple Intelligence and a revamped Siri are the headline additions, the speed gains target everyday responsiveness on existing iPhones when the update ships later this year.

title: Amazon unveils new AI agents, trying to thread the needle between autonomy and human control
blurb: Amazon introduced new AWS AI agents at its New York Summit that act autonomously on tasks like patching security vulnerabilities and triaging email. The release pairs that autonomy with configurable human-in-the-loop controls, letting customers cap how far an agent goes without approval as AWS pushes deeper into the production-agent race against Microsoft and Google.

title: Show HN: High-Res Neural Cellular Automata
blurb: Neural cellular automata pair a coarse-grid NCA with a lightweight implicit decoder to render self-organizing patterns at arbitrary resolution in real time.

title: An ALS patient just logged 3,800 hours speaking through a brain implant at home — and the researchers are quietly calling him something the field has never had before
blurb: An ALS patient logged 3,800 hours of speech through a brain-computer interface at home, the longest sustained real-world use of a speech BCI on record. The implant decodes signals from his motor cortex into synthesized words, and the months of uninterrupted at-home data give researchers their first look at how such a device holds up outside the lab.

title: U.S. science is in chaos
blurb: Federal cuts have thrown U.S. basic research into disarray, with NASA losing roughly a fifth of its workforce to DOGE-pushed buyouts and the administration's budget zeroing out programs like the $1B AXIS x-ray telescope. The article argues the postwar compact between science and politics has broken, leaving roughly 40% of basic research funding, the share that comes from the federal government, exposed to abrupt political reversal.

title: Epic Games details how it's embracing generative AI in Unreal Engine
blurb: Epic Games detailed plans to embed generative AI across upcoming versions of Unreal Engine, building the tooling directly into the editor for asset and content creation. The move pushes AI-assisted workflows toward the center of mainstream game development, where Unreal already powers a large share of studios alongside its use in film and simulation.

title: Roelof Botha joins SpaceX’s board of directors
blurb: Roelof Botha joined SpaceX's board of directors, less than a week after the company completed the largest IPO ever. The former Sequoia managing partner, who Elon Musk first hired to run PayPal's finance division in 2000, fills a vacancy and joins the audit committee, bringing the board to nine directors at a company where Musk controls over 80% of voting power.

title: US Looks to Expand Nuclear Power as AI Drives Up Energy Demand
blurb: The US Nuclear Regulatory Commission has reformed its licensing framework and inspection processes as the Trump administration pushes to expand nuclear power amid surging AI-driven electricity demand. NRC chair Ho K. Nieh said the agency remains a "strong regulator" despite the changes but declined to specify which rules are under review.

title: Mini data centres may be coming to residential homes. Here’s how NVIDIA is working to power AI with domestic electricity in the US
blurb: NVIDIA is partnering with home-electrification firms Span and XFRA to explore mini data centers powered by residential electricity in the US. The effort aims to tap distributed home energy and smart panels to support AI compute, easing pressure on strained grids and centralized data center capacity as demand for inference keeps climbing.

title: Sony’s new LYTIA L910 camera sensor will turbocharge night shots and 4K capture on phones
blurb: Sony announced the LYTIA L910, a 50MP phone sensor with 100 dB dynamic range and 4K/60fps HDR capture, shipping summer 2026.

title: Epic Games says Unreal Engine 6 will help developers "build content faster" using AI models
blurb: Epic Games detailed Unreal Engine 6, which merges Unreal Engine with Unreal Editor for Fortnite and puts LLMs and generative AI at the core, including an MCP with Claude, Gemini, and Codex integrations to speed content creation. The engine also moves gameplay programming to Verse and adds cross-game portability, with early access targeted for late 2027 and full release 12 to 18 months later.

title: Nvidia-backed optics vendor to boost wafer output by 4x to meet AI interconnect demand
blurb: An Nvidia-backed optics maker plans to quadruple its wafer output to keep pace with surging demand for AI interconnects.
