blurb: This paper introduces Turing-RL, a reinforcement learning method that trains LLM user simulators using a discriminative Turing reward, where an LLM judge scores how indistinguishable a generated response is from the real user's given their history. Across conversational chat and Reddit forum discussion, optimizing for indistinguishability rather than matching a single ground truth response outperformed baselines on both LLM and human evaluation metrics.

title: Transformer Geometry Observatory TGO-I: Spectral Geometry Observatory
blurb: This paper introduces the Transformer Geometry Observatory (TGO), a framework for analyzing the representational geometry of Vision Transformers, with its first installment tracking spectral metrics like Effective Rank, Participation Ratio, and Spectral Entropy across training of a ViT-Small/16 on ImageNet-100. Contrary to the intuition that training concentrates information into a few dominant directions, the authors find variance progressively redistributes across dimensions, most prominently in the final CLS token representation.

title: Enhancing Multilingual Reasoning via Steerable Model Merging
blurb: This paper introduces ST-Merge, a steerable model merging framework that adaptively weights a multilingual model and a reasoning model rather than fusing them with a single fixed strategy. A gated cross-attention mechanism filters the two source models per input, and the method outperforms strong baselines across four multilingual reasoning benchmarks spanning 21 languages.

title: Finding Compiler-Platform Interaction Bugs in Deep Learning Pipelines via Cross-Layer Constraints
blurb: This paper introduces XCheck, an automated testing framework for deep learning compilers like TVM and ONNX-MLIR that targets compiler-platform interaction bugs missed by type-constraint-based approaches. By deriving full-stack constraints to guide model generation and inserting assertions for behavior equivalence partitioning, XCheck found 2,034 bug-revealing cases across three compilers, including memory overflows, integer overflows, and silent unexpected compilations.

title: Collecting robot training data is dirty, unglamorous work. Some AI labs are already paying XDOF to do it.
blurb: XDOF emerged from stealth with $70M from Thrive Capital, Spark Capital, a16z, Lux, and WndrCo to build the data pipelines, collection tools, and annotation systems frontier labs need to train robots on physical interaction. The startup already works with 20 customers, including several unnamed AI labs, and is betting that the next AI bottleneck is robot training data rather than models or chips.

title: As tech giants rush to build AI infrastructure, some residents living near data centers say a constant infrasonic vibration is ruining their health and homes (Adeel Hassan/New York Times)
blurb: Residents living near AI data centers report that constant infrasonic vibration from the facilities is harming their health and damaging their homes. The complaints surface a physical externality of the data center buildout, as tech giants site large-scale compute capacity closer to residential areas to keep pace with AI demand.

title: NeuralTrust, which offers tech that lets enterprises discover, monitor, govern, and secure AI agents, raised a $20M seed led by Alstin Capital (Tamara Djurickovic/Tech.eu)
blurb: NeuralTrust raised a $20M seed round led by Alstin Capital to build tooling that lets enterprises discover, monitor, govern, and secure AI agents. The platform targets the operational gap created as companies deploy autonomous agents into production, where visibility and access controls remain limited.

title: Beyond Tokenization: Direct Timestep Embedding and Contrastive Alignment for Time-Series Question Answering
blurb: CADE (Contrastive Alignment with Direct Embedding) is a time-series question answering framework that maps each timestep directly into an LLM's embedding space via a point-wise linear encoder and MLP projector, avoiding the tokenization and patching bottlenecks that lose magnitude and trend information. It adds a one-directional supervised contrastive loss to align time-series embeddings with frozen class-name text anchors, outperforming open-source and proprietary baselines across six tasks on the Time-MQA benchmark.

title: Trump Says Anthropic Negotiations Continue as AI Leaders Huddle at G-7
blurb: President Trump said negotiations with Anthropic are progressing during a Group of Seven summit, even as a shutdown of the company's AI models continues to drag on. Some world leaders at the summit raised concerns about losing access to leading AI tools, underscoring how dependent foreign governments have become on a small number of US providers.

title: DOJ Defends Musk’s xAI in Data-Center Pollution Lawsuit
blurb: The Justice Department filed in support of Musk's xAI in a lawsuit over pollution from the company's Mississippi data center, arguing the facility is training AI models the government relies on for national-security purposes. The intervention signals the federal government is willing to invoke national security to shield private AI infrastructure from environmental litigation.

title: Google prepares Personalization and AI Editing for NotebookLM
blurb: Google is preparing two NotebookLM features, Personal Intelligence and AI Editing, both spotted in testing ahead of an expected rollout. Personal Intelligence gives the tool memory of past conversations confined to NotebookLM activity rather than Gmail or Docs, while AI Editing lets users select note text, send it to chat, and have NotebookLM rework saved responses.

title: Convey closes on $38M round to help companies automate repetitive work with AI teammates
blurb: Convey raised $38M in a Series A led by Andreessen Horowitz, with Khosla Ventures and Pear VC participating, to scale a platform that lets nontechnical workers build and manage autonomous AI teammates. The company says new agents can be onboarded in about three hours and then take over tasks like processing invoices, reconciling financial data, and managing campaign reports.

title: AI cyber warfare startup Twenty raises $100M at $1B valuation
blurb: Twenty raised $100M at a $1B valuation in a Series B led by Accel to expand its AI-powered cyber warfare software for the U.S. military and intelligence community. Founded in 2024 by former public-sector security experts and ex-Palo Alto Networks leaders, the company automates tasks that can take humans weeks while keeping manual evaluation in the loop.

title: OpenAI launches scheduled tasks in ChatGPT, details here
blurb: OpenAI launched scheduled tasks in ChatGPT, letting users set up automated, recurring prompts that run at specified times. The feature began rolling out today and lets ChatGPT deliver reminders, briefings, and other routine outputs on a defined schedule without manual re-prompting.

title: Amazon unveils new AI agents, trying to thread the needle between autonomy and human control
blurb: AWS used its New York Summit to release AI agents that act autonomously on tasks like patching security vulnerabilities and triaging email, with controls that cap how far each agent can go without sign-off. The launch reflects Amazon's bid to balance hands-off automation against the oversight enterprise customers still demand before delegating sensitive work.

title: Pramaana Labs raises $27M to make AI prove its answers
blurb: Pramaana Labs raised $27M in seed funding for a system it calls a compiler for high-stakes AI, which checks a model's answer against a domain's rules and withholds it unless a proof engine can verify it as correct. Built on Lean, the open-source proof language, the product targets regulated work like tax, medical diagnosis, cybersecurity, and financial compliance.

title: AI operating system startup Conduct closes £45m round
blurb: Conduct raised $60M (£44.72M) in a Series A co-led by Index Ventures and ICONIQ, with strategic investment from SAP, Creandum, Lucid Capital, and Booom. The startup's "AI operating system" maps how custom code, configuration dependencies, and integrations connect to business logic, aiming to make decades-old enterprise systems legible enough for AI agents to act on.

title: NeSyCat Torch: A Differentiable Tensor Implementation of Categorical Semantics for Neurosymbolic Learning
blurb: NeSyCat Torch is a differentiable tensor implementation that unifies classical, fuzzy, probabilistic, and neural truth semantics under one monad-parametric framework, interpreting learned predicates and functions via neural networks. On MNIST addition, the HaskTorch, JAX, and PyTorch backends beat LTN and DeepProbLog on speed and accuracy and approach DeepStochLog, while staying in a single framework that generalizes across first-order neurosymbolic approaches.

title: A Multi-Domain Benchmark for Detecting AI-Generated Text-Rich Images from GPT-Image-2
blurb: This paper introduces a multi-domain benchmark for detecting AI-generated text-rich images from OpenAI's GPT Image 2, spanning 8,602 images across six categories including commercial posters, infographics, receipts, tables, and UI screenshots. Zero-shot evaluation of five detectors shows performance is highly domain-dependent, with even the strongest conventional detector breaking down under JPEG compression.

title: OneCanvas: 3D Scene Understanding via Panoramic Reprojection
blurb: OneCanvas is a 3D scene understanding method that unprojects patch features from all views onto a single equirectangular panoramic canvas, letting a pretrained VLM consume the result as an ordinary image with no geometry encoders or architectural changes. It reaches state-of-the-art accuracy on SQA3D and VSI-Bench and generalizes to out-of-distribution data on SPBench while using an order of magnitude less training compute than the strongest competing methods.

title: OrthoReg: Orthogonal Regularization for Hybrid Symbolic-Neural Dynamical Systems
blurb: OrthoReg is an orthogonal regularization method for hybrid symbolic-neural models of dynamical systems that penalizes overlap between the symbolic and neural components, preventing the network from relearning physics the symbolic part already captures. On benchmarks with partial library mismatch, it yields a complementary decomposition that improves symbolic recovery and out-of-distribution behavior over standard L2 regularization.

title: AGDN: Learning to Solve Traveling Salesman Problem with Anisotropic Graph Diffusion Network
blurb: This paper introduces the Anisotropic Graph Diffusion Network (AGDN), a graph neural network for solving the Traveling Salesman Problem that builds a MixScore transition matrix combining node similarity with pairwise distance to avoid losing optimal-tour edges during graph sparsification. AGDN uses anisotropic diffusion for multi-hop information exchange, outperforms existing methods at competitive runtimes, and generalizes to problem sizes and node distributions beyond its training set.

title: When AUC Misleads: Polarization-Aware Evaluation of Deepfake Detectors under Domain Shift
blurb: This paper argues that evaluating deepfake detectors with per-dataset AUC misses real-world conditions, where detectors face mixed data sources and varying artifact types at once. The authors introduce Cross-AUC, a metric that averages per-domain AUCs while factoring in prediction polarization measured by the Wasserstein distance between class score distributions, with experiments on seven benchmark datasets.

title: SenFlow: Inter-Sentence Flow Modeling for AI-Generated Text Detection in Hybrid Documents
blurb: SenFlow recasts sentence-level AI-text detection in human-LLM co-authored documents as structured prediction, combining graph-based inter-sentence propagation with linear-chain CRF decoding in a single document-level pass. It reaches state-of-the-art results on MOSAIC, a new 16,000-document benchmark generated by DeepSeek-V3.2 and Kimi K2, with a 4.15 pp Macro-F1 gain on the hardest cross-domain transfer setting.

title: Show HN: High-Res Neural Cellular Automata
blurb: This paper introduces a method for rendering Neural Cellular Automata at arbitrary resolution by pairing a coarse-grid NCA with a lightweight implicit decoder that maps cell states and local coordinates to appearance attributes. Because both the NCA updates and the decoder are local, inference stays highly parallelizable, and experiments across 2D/3D grids and mesh domains produce high-resolution morphogenesis and texture synthesis in real-time.

title: RECOM: A Validity Discrimination Tradeoff in Automatic Metrics for Open Ended Reddit Question Answering
blurb: RECOM is a contamination-free evaluation dataset of 15,000 r/AskReddit questions paired with authentic community replies that postdate every tested model's training cutoff, used to probe automatic metrics for open-ended question answering. The authors find a validity-discrimination tradeoff baked into the metrics themselves: cosine similarity separates real from random answers (Cohen's d around 2) but cannot rank five 7-10B models, while BERTScore's apparent ranking collapses once response length is controlled.

title: Runtime Compliance Verification for AI Agents
blurb: This paper introduces C-Trace, a framework that encodes GDPR rules like consent, purpose limitation, data minimization, and the right to erasure as formal predicates over an AI agent's execution traces. A runtime monitor intercepts every tool call and model output to reject non-compliant actions, holding attack success rate at or below 12% under 10% extractor noise and reaching 0% with perfect extraction.

title: US Looks to Expand Nuclear Power as AI Drives Up Energy Demand
blurb: The US Nuclear Regulatory Commission has reformed its framework for licensing plants and conducting regulatory checks as the Trump administration pushes to expand nuclear energy amid rising AI power demand. NRC chair Ho K. Nieh said the agency remains a strong regulator and could not yet disclose which specific regulations are under review.

title: Signature filtering: a lightweight enhancement for statistical watermark detection in large language models
blurb: This paper introduces signature filtering, a detection-time module that strengthens statistical watermark detection in LLM outputs without changing how watermarks are embedded or text is generated. It learns a small set of "signature" tokens that make watermark tests unreliable and removes them before detection, raising detection rates in weak-signal and low-entropy settings from 8-31% to 78-99% across four watermark families, six models, and four corpora.

title: Where Did the Variability Go? From Vibe Coding to Product Lines by Regeneration
blurb: This paper examines what happens to software variability in vibe coding, where an LLM generates an entire program from a natural language prompt. An analysis of 10 vibe-coded C/C++ projects found near-zero in-artifact variability, since all configuration decisions get resolved at generation time. The authors propose Variability by Regeneration, a product-line approach where the LLM generates a separate dead-code-free binary for each variant from a declarative spec, with a dispatcher routing requests to the matching build.

title: Trade-offs in Medical LLM Adaptation: An Empirical Study in French QA
blurb: This paper compares continual pretraining (CPT), supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and their combination for adapting LLMs to French medical question-answering across three model families, sizes, and initialization types. For multiple-choice QA, CPT+SFT usually scores best but rarely beats SFT significantly, making SFT a cost-effective default, while CPT helps open-ended generation and SFT often degrades it, with French adaptation transferring to English benchmarks.

title: Anthropic Gets A Warning, SpaceX Shares Fall on Fourth Day | Bloomberg Tech 6/17/2026
blurb: The US Commerce Secretary warned Anthropic that it needs government permission before granting foreign nationals access to its most advanced AI models. The warning signals tighter federal control over frontier model access, arriving as SpaceX shares fell on their fourth trading day after three days of gains and Apple prepared camera-equipped AirPods for a late-2027 launch.

title: Vibe-decoding the White House-Anthropic fight over Fable
blurb: The White House placed export control restrictions on Anthropic's Mythos 5 and Fable models on Friday, forcing the company to cut off access for foreign governments and nationals. This column argues that the chaos stems from competing factions inside the Trump administration leaking rival narratives, leaving it unclear who was responsible for what could have been a major cybersecurity failure.

title: China pitches free AI for the developing world as the G7 debates who gets access to American models
blurb: China's top diplomat Wang Yi called for a global AI cooperation organization open to all countries, pairing the pitch with cheap and freely downloadable models like DeepSeek and Qwen for the Global South. The move landed as the G7 wrapped its summit discussing "trusted partner" access to US models, formalizing two diverging governance blocs around open weights versus export-controlled subscriptions.

title: OpenAI kauft das Kieler KI-Startup Ona
blurb: OpenAI acquired Ona, a Kiel-based startup founded in 2020 that builds secure, reproducible cloud development environments, in what is reported as one of the largest German exits in recent years. The deal supports Codex, OpenAI's coding assistant now at over 5M weekly users and up 400% since January, by giving agents a persistent place to run long workflows inside a company's own infrastructure.

title: Reference-Driven Multi-Speaker Audio Scene Generation from In-the-Wild Priors
blurb: ScenA conditions a text-to-audio flow-matching foundation model on multiple reference voices and a free-form scene prompt, generating multi-speaker conversations with overlapping speech, ambient noise, and paralinguistic events without any per-turn structure. To stop the model from matching speakers by acoustic similarity instead of the prompt, the authors use a high-noise-biased timestep distribution, beating prior systems on speaker-binding metrics on the CoVoMix2-Dialogue benchmark.

title: SCAN: Enhance Time Series Anomaly Detection via Multi-Scale Neighborhood-Centered Clustering
blurb: This paper introduces SCAN, a method that adds multi-scale clustering to reconstruction-based time series anomaly detection to balance the over-generalization and under-generalization problems that plague the mainstream approach. It constrains reconstruction toward cluster-center representations of normal patterns and pairs reconstruction error with a cluster-membership confidence score, reporting state-of-the-art results across real-world datasets from multiple domains.

title: P-K-GCN: Physics-augmented Koopman-enhanced Graph Convolutional Network for Deep Spatiotemporal Super-resolution
blurb: This paper introduces P-K-GCN, a graph convolutional network for spatiotemporal super-resolution that reconstructs high-resolution simulations from coarse inputs on irregular geometries. It combines a spline-based GCN with Koopman operator theory to linearize temporal dynamics and adds a physics-based loss, which the authors prove tightens generalization bounds and reduces error when reconstructing cardiac electrodynamics across a 3D heart.

title: Acceleration of an algebraic multigrid pressure solver using graph neural networks
blurb: This paper introduces a data-driven algebraic multigrid smoother that uses a modified graph convolutional isomorphism network to predict optimal polynomial coefficients for a sparse pseudo-inverse operator, targeting the pressure-Poisson bottleneck in incompressible flow solvers. The method cuts the V-cycles needed to hit a given tolerance, delivers wall-clock speedups of 4% to 37% across benchmarks, and generalizes to meshes up to 128 times larger than those seen in training.

title: Freeing the Law with LOCUS: A Local Ordinance Corpus for the United States
blurb: This paper introduces LOCUS, a machine-readable corpus of U.S. local ordinance codes covering nearly all publicly available municipal and county law, with raw text from 9,239 cities and counties and a harmonized access layer spanning the 2,309 largest counties. The authors used OCR to consolidate fragmented vendor formats and trained ModernBERT-based classifiers to score local law on dimensions like opacity and paternalism that had not been studied at this scale.

title: From Specification to Execution: AI Assisted Scientific Workflow Management
blurb: This paper presents a specification-driven system that generates, debugs, and runs scientific workflows, integrating Pegasus with an MCP layer.

title: The Gate Is Only as Honest as Its Contracts: ContractGuard for the Contract Layer of Risk-Aware Causal Gating
blurb: ContractGuard is a verifier sitting between the tool registry and the gate in Risk-Aware Causal Gating, which defends tool-augmented LLM agents against indirect prompt injection by hiding dangerous tools. The authors show effect forgery, not risk-label tampering, is the load-bearing attack, and ContractGuard's signed provenance, typed attestation, and runtime effect verification drive injection success to zero across six hosted models.

title: SpaceX to buy Cursor AI coding agent operator Anysphere for $60B
blurb: SpaceX agreed to buy Anysphere, the maker of the Cursor AI coding agent, for $60B, pulling a frontier dev-tools company under a hardware and aerospace umbrella. The deal signals that the buyers with the deepest pockets for AI talent and tooling are no longer the cloud and software incumbents but cash-rich operators from adjacent industries. For founders building on coding agents, it also concentrates one of the category leaders inside a company with its own engineering agenda, raising the odds that Cursor's roadmap starts serving its parent before the broader market.

title: The State of Fable, The Jailbreak Problem, SpaceX Acquires Cursor
blurb: Ben Thompson's read is that the administration is very likely wrong about Fable, but he puts the burden of fixing that on Anthropic rather than the regulators. The argument is that when policymakers misjudge a frontier lab's model, the lab owns the failure to explain itself, because perception is part of the product. For founders building on these labs, it's a reminder that technical correctness doesn't settle the question. The party that controls the narrative around capability and risk is the one that has to do the communicating, not the one that happens to be right.

title: Databricks sales growth tops 80%, but margin are shrinking from swarm of AI agents
blurb: Databricks's annualized revenue jumped over 80% to $6.9 billion, passing Snowflake and lifting its private valuation to $134 billion, but its margins are sliding as customers lean on AI agents. Each agent generates far more queries against frontier models, so consumption climbs while the cost of serving it eats into gross margin. AI products now bring in $1.7 billion a year, and the new pattern is "value-maxxing": companies have stopped pushing maximum token use and are tightening budgets, which is exactly why Databricks built a gateway to flag spend before it runs over.

title: Google Gemini co-lead Noam Shazeer leaves for OpenAI
blurb: Noam Shazeer, Google's VP of engineering and a co-lead on Gemini, is leaving for OpenAI less than two years after Google brought him back as part of its Character.AI deal. The move strips Google of one of its most senior model architects weeks after it shipped Gemini 3.5 Flash, and it lands as OpenAI confidentially files for an IPO. The talent war is now the main front in the model race, and the labs are paying whatever it takes to pull the handful of people who can actually push a frontier model forward.

title: 80% of ChatGPT product recommendations change when search is enabled: Study
blurb: Turning on web search reshuffled 80% of ChatGPT's product recommendations across a 20,000-response study from Visibility Labs, leaving only 19.8% of search-off picks still standing when search was enabled. The products ChatGPT named every single time without search held up the worst, with just 15.8% surviving the switch. For any founder banking on AI recommendations as a discovery channel, the lesson is that visibility hinges on what's in ChatGPT's cited sources, not on whatever the base model has memorized.

title: AI Startup Midjourney Pivots to Health With Ultrasound Machine
blurb: Midjourney, best known for AI image generation, announced its first hardware product and a move into personal health and medical devices, starting with an ultrasound machine shown at a San Francisco event. The pivot pushes a software-only company into regulated medical hardware, a market with FDA clearance timelines and manufacturing costs that have little in common with shipping a generative model. It is a reminder that the AI labs with strong brands and cash are willing to leave their original category entirely when they see a bigger opening.

title: How Stripe, Google, Canva, Cloudflare and Higgsfield Are Actually Selling in 2026
blurb: Six wildly different companies — Stripe, Google, Canva, Cloudflare, Owner, and Higgsfield — landed on the same go-to-market playbook in 2026 without coordinating. Build and sell in parallel, centralize your AI intelligence, treat agents as a real customer, and charge for outcomes instead of seats. The numbers behind it explain the urgency: top AI companies grew 175% in 2026, Lovable hit $400M sixteen months in, and Stripe's data shows top consumers now spend $371 on AI, more than the average American pays for internet, streaming, and phone combined.

title: Anthropic got hit by export rules nobody understands
blurb: The Trump administration ordered Anthropic to cut off all foreign nationals, including users and employees inside the US, forcing the company to pull Fable 5 and Mythos 5 offline for everyone this week. The legal hook is novel: export controls have always governed things that cross borders, like weapons, source code, or model weights, but here nothing is being shipped because the models stay on Anthropic's servers and users only get chat responses. Experts call it unprecedented, and it signals that AI governance now runs on opaque, ad hoc orders any model provider could be hit with next.

title: PsiQuantum breaks ground in Australia on what it says will be the world’s first utility-scale quantum computer
blurb: PsiQuantum broke ground in Queensland on what it calls the world's first utility-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computer, backed by nearly $1B in Australian and US government money plus a $7B private valuation. The site will eventually hold tens of thousands of photonic quantum chips, betting on light-through-silicon instead of the superconducting designs IBM and Google use. No company has yet demonstrated fault-tolerant quantum at commercial scale, and PsiQuantum has given no timeline for when the full machine goes live.

title: SpaceX leapfrogs Amazon and briefly tops Microsoft in market value on Cursor acquisition news
blurb: SpaceX briefly topped Microsoft and passed Amazon to become the world's fourth-most valuable public company, hitting roughly $2.94T less than a week after its $75B IPO. The jump followed its $60B acquisition of Cursor, the AI coding startup running past $1B in annualized revenue, as Musk moves to build a vertically integrated AI stack spanning chips, data centers, software, networks, and space. Retail demand is doing much of the lifting, with Vanda Research pegging SpaceX at about three-quarters of all single-stock retail purchases on Monday.

title: Helion secures world’s first regulatory licenses for fusion power plant being built in Washington
blurb: Helion Energy became the first company in the world to receive regulatory licenses for a fusion power plant, with Washington's Department of Health issuing the Radioactive Materials and Radioactive Air Emissions permits for its Orion facility in Malaga. The 50-megawatt plant sits next to a Microsoft data center it has agreed to power by 2028, and Helion just raised $463 million, bringing total funding to $1.5 billion. No one has yet produced commercially viable fusion energy, but the regulatory path now exists, after the NRC ruled in 2023 that fusion should be treated more like a particle accelerator than a fission reactor.

title: NASA picks Eric Schmidt’s rocket company for Mars mission, setting up a race with SpaceX
blurb: NASA hired Relativity Space, the rocket maker Eric Schmidt bought last year, to build and fly the Aeolus spacecraft to Mars orbit by 2028. The deal copies the model NASA used with SpaceX for ISS cargo and Firefly for Moon landers: the agency owns the science, the company provides low-cost infrastructure and shoulders part of the development cost. The bet is unproven, since Relativity has yet to reach orbit and past startup partners have gone bankrupt or crashed landers, but it sets up an Schmidt-versus-Musk race to the Red Planet.

title: Parafin lands a Goldman Sachs credit facility to embed lending inside Amazon, DoorDash, and Walmart
blurb: Parafin secured a Goldman Sachs-led credit facility to scale embedded small business lending across Amazon, DoorDash, Walmart, and TikTok Shop, where sellers get financing built into the platform they already run on rather than applying to a bank. The model has funded more than 50,000 businesses and extended over $35B in offers across the US and Canada, with the majority going to repeat borrowers. The signal for founders is that distribution is moving inside the workflow, and the lending now attaches to the platform a business lives in, not the bank it visits.

title: SpaceX to acquire the AI coding startup Cursor for $60 billion
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire AI coding startup Cursor for $60B in stock, days after the biggest IPO ever, sending its shares up 16% to make it the fourth most valuable US company. The deal folds Cursor into xAI to take on Anthropic and OpenAI in coding, but Cursor's market share has already slid from 41% to 26% in the past year while Anthropic now controls half the category. SpaceX is paying a premium for momentum that is fading, betting frontier AI distribution matters more than the share it bought.

title: World leaders want American AI. They just don’t want America to be able to turn it off.
blurb: The U.S. just showed it can revoke access to American AI overnight, and the world's governments noticed. After the Trump administration blocked Anthropic from exporting its newest models on national security grounds, G7 leaders including Macron and Modi warned that any country or company building on U.S. AI infrastructure now carries kill-switch risk it can't control. The response is already forming: G7 leaders floated a "trusted partners" scheme to route access around U.S. restrictions, and sovereignty, not price, is becoming the deciding factor in who builds on whose models.

title: AI is hurting Apple in more ways than one: it may force iPhone price increases
blurb: AI's memory-chip demand has driven a fourfold jump in costs since last year, and Tim Cook now calls iPhone, Mac, and iPad price increases "unavoidable." TechInsights estimates Apple would need to add $270 to the next iPhone Pro, already starting at $1,099, just to hold its margin. The squeeze cuts deeper because AI hasn't paid off for Apple yet, and more on-device Siri processing means more memory, pointing the same direction: consumers covering the bill.

title: What the Anthropic shutdown means for Europe
blurb: The US government ordered Anthropic to suspend access to its top models for foreign nationals, and Anthropic responded by cutting off all customers, including those in the US, to stay compliant. It is the first real demonstration of a digital "kill switch," with Washington able to disrupt or shut off technologies that European organizations now lean on to run critical services. The restrictions may be temporary, but they hand European founders a concrete reason to weigh sovereignty and supplier concentration before building core operations on a single US AI provider.

title: Microsoft Makes Big AI Inroads in China by Selling OpenAI Models
blurb: Microsoft has built a sizable business selling OpenAI models to Chinese companies, even as Washington and Beijing harden their rivalry over AI. The deals run through Azure, which gives Chinese firms a compliant path to frontier models they can't easily buy elsewhere, and gives Microsoft a revenue line most of its US peers have written off. The signal for founders is that the export-control map has gaps, and a distribution partner with the right legal footing can sell into markets the model labs themselves won't touch directly.

title: US pulls the 'kill-switch' on Anthropic's Fable 5 AI models, sending global allies scrambling — European and Canadian leaders alarm allies over sudden export bans
blurb: The Trump administration blocked exports of Anthropic's Mythos 5 and Fable 5 frontier models, cutting off allied governments from direct access overnight. European and Canadian leaders are treating the move as a sovereignty risk, since a model their economies now depend on can be switched off by a foreign policy decision. The likely result is a push to fund national frontier alternatives, even where the local compute and talent aren't there yet, because the cost of being dependent just became clearer than the cost of building.

title: AI referrals to travel sites surge 194% as engagement rises: Adobe
blurb: AI referrals to U.S. travel sites grew 194% year over year in May and are up 2,215% since Adobe started tracking in October 2024, with AI-referred visitors spending 70% longer on site, bouncing 41% less, and engaging 21% more than traditional traffic. They still convert 28% less, but that gap has narrowed nearly 70% since 2024 as planning moves from research into booking. The lever now is readability, because more than a third of content on some leading travel pages is still unreadable to LLMs, and structured property and product details are what AI systems actually parse.

title: After unveiling ridiculously expensive AR glasses, Snap’s stock takes a dive
blurb: Snap launched Specs, its long-awaited AR glasses, at nearly $2,200 a unit, and the stock fell more than 5% the next morning on top of a 30% slide over the past year. CEO Evan Spiegel framed the price as comparable to a high-end laptop, positioning Specs between Meta's cheaper Ray-Bans and Apple's pricier Vision Pro. The problem is the math: Snap's core users are teenagers, who don't carry $2,200 for a phone accessory, which leaves the profitability path for a decade-long bet looking unclear.

title: World model maker Odyssey nabs $1.45B valuation backed by Amazon and other big names
blurb: Odyssey, a world model startup founded by self-driving veterans Oliver Cameron and Jeff Hawke, raised a $310M Series B at a $1.45B valuation, led by Natural Capital with Amazon, AMD Ventures, and GV in. World models gather data from the physical world and simulate it with accurate physics, the bet for what comes after text and chat, and Odyssey already ships models for video-game creation, robotics, and interactive video from prompts. Part of the deal is strategic: AWS becomes its preferred cloud and Odyssey will optimize for Amazon's Trainium chips, a direct shot at Nvidia.

title: Anthropic Ban Forces Investor Rethink of Political Risk
blurb: Political risk has jumped to the top of the list for AI investors, and it now ranks alongside stretched valuations and runaway capex as a threat to the rally. A government ban on Anthropic showed how fast a regulatory or political move can wipe out value that fundamentals say should be safe. For founders raising into this market, it means the diligence questions are shifting from burn and growth toward exposure to policy, procurement rules, and whichever way the political wind turns next.

title: Star Google Researcher Joins OpenAI in Coup for ChatGPT Creator
blurb: One of Google's most prominent AI researchers is leaving for OpenAI, a direct hit to Alphabet in the multibillion-dollar race to build the most capable frontier models. The talent war is now the main lever, with the scarce resource being not compute or data but the few dozen people who can actually push the research frontier. When one researcher's move counts as a corporate coup, the moat isn't the model, it's the roster, and that roster is liquid in a way capital can't fully fix.

title: Apple to Raise Prices Due to Memory Chip Crunch, Tim Cook Says
blurb: Apple will raise prices across its lineup because surging memory chip costs have made the increases "unavoidable," CEO Tim Cook told the Journal. The crunch traces back to AI data center buildouts soaking up DRAM and NAND supply, and Apple's scale no longer guarantees first claim on the cheapest components. For any founder shipping hardware or running memory-heavy infrastructure, the signal is that the AI compute boom is now bleeding into consumer pricing, and the component costs you budgeted six months ago are no longer a safe assumption.

title: Meta expands live shopping ads and virtual card checkout to drive more purchases
blurb: Meta is folding commerce deeper into Facebook and Instagram, expanding Live Video Ads globally, making product feeds a default input to all Sales campaigns, and adding a virtual card checkout with Mastercard and Visa that hides real card numbers from merchants. The play is to close the loop on AI-driven product discovery so the purchase never leaves the app. For brands selling on Meta, that means less control over the checkout and the catalog, as Meta's AI increasingly assembles which product and creative each shopper sees.

title: Businesses have started using Kalshi to hedge business risks; Kalshi says institutional trading volume on its platform has grown 800% since November 2025 (Michael J. de la Merced/New York Times)
blurb: Prediction markets are becoming a corporate hedging tool, with Kalshi reporting that institutional trading volume on its platform has grown 800% since November 2025. Businesses are using event contracts to hedge against risks that traditional derivatives don't cleanly cover, from weather to policy outcomes to commodity shocks. The shift hints at a new layer of corporate risk management forming outside the usual insurance and futures markets, and if it holds, founders may soon treat prediction markets as a real line item rather than a curiosity.

title: A Blackstone-led consortium agrees to take control of software company Medallia from Thoma Bravo, which will lose the entire $5B it invested in Medallia (Financial Times)
blurb: Thoma Bravo will lose the entire $5B it sank into Medallia as a Blackstone-led consortium takes control of the experience-management software company. The deal marks one of the largest single-investment write-offs for a major private equity firm, which took Medallia private in 2021 at the top of the software-valuation cycle. With rates higher and SaaS multiples compressed, even well-capitalized PE sponsors are now eating total losses on deals that looked safe three years ago.

title: Anthropic asked for regulation. Washington went much further
blurb: Anthropic spent years lobbying for binding AI regulation, and the Trump administration just used that opening against it. Days after CEO Dario Amodei argued that unsafe frontier models should be blockable like uncertified airplanes, the company received an export control directive suspending access to its newest Claude models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, for any foreign national including its own employees. The lesson for founders is that inviting the state to police your industry hands it a lever it can pull on terms you don't control, even at a near-$1 trillion valuation.

title: SpaceX's all-stock Cursor deal illustrates the power of public markets, which could help SpaceX narrow the gap with Anthropic and OpenAI through acquisitions (New York Times)
blurb: SpaceX is paying for its Cursor deal entirely in stock, using its own richly valued equity as acquisition currency rather than cash. That move shows how a company sitting on a high private valuation can buy its way toward the frontier, and it gives SpaceX a path to close the AI gap with Anthropic and OpenAI through acquisitions instead of building everything in-house. Expect more deals priced in paper than in dollars as the labs with the strongest valuations consolidate the talent and tooling everyone else is still trying to assemble.

title: TikTok Shop Bans AI Voices, Prerecorded Audio From Promotional Livestreams
blurb: TikTok Shop has banned AI-generated voices, prerecorded audio, and static-image content from promotional livestreams and shoppable videos, requiring all verbal communication to happen in real time. The move runs against ByteDance's own push to sell advertisers AI creative tools through Symphony, but the commerce data explains the split. eMarketer projects TikTok Shop will hit $23.4B in US sales in 2026, up 48% year over year, and found consumers who notice AI in brand marketing are four times more likely to trust the brand less. In live commerce, a real human is still the conversion engine.

title: Amazon Launches Program for Independent Creators Navigating AI Search
blurb: Amazon launched Amazon Content Partners, a free program that gives independent creators control over how AI crawlers access and monetize their content. It bundles AI traffic management through AWS WAF, a 1% Amazon Associates commission boost, premium ad access, and $100 a month in AWS hosting credits. The program is in gated preview for U.S. creators, with general availability planned later this summer.

title: CEOs of Anthropic and Google DeepMind call for U.S.-led AI coalition in meeting at G7
blurb: The CEOs of Anthropic and Google DeepMind used a closed-door G7 lunch with Trump and a dozen tech executives to push for a U.S.-led coalition that sets global rules on AI. Dario Amodei proposed structured access to frontier models and China-excluded trade in chips and critical components, plus cooperation on cyber, bioterror, and intelligence risks, while Sam Altman called for an international forum to set testing standards. The pitch lands as Anthropic negotiates with the administration over export controls that just forced it to disable access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5.

title: Series D funding rises 308% in first half of 2026
blurb: European Series D funding climbed 308% in the first half of 2026, the clearest sign yet that late-stage capital is flowing back after a long drought. The recovery is concentrated at the top of the stack, where investors are again willing to write nine-figure checks into proven companies rather than spread smaller bets across early rounds. For founders sitting on Series C cash, the implication is that the late-stage market has reopened, and the companies raising now are the ones with the metrics to justify the round.

title: A Colombian AI startup wants to assist half of Latin America’s doctors. Andreessen Horowitz just backed it.
blurb: Telepatia, a Colombian AI clinical assistant, raised $33M in a Series A led by a16z, with total funding now $42M and 14M patients reached across five countries in under a year. The product transcribes consultations in real time, flags drug interactions, and surfaces guideline-based suggestions, giving doctors at one Brazilian hospital back 1.7 hours a day. The bet fits the market: Latin American physicians spend 40% to 70% of their time on documentation, and the company aims to reach half the region's 1.9M doctors by the end of 2027.

title: Behavox raises $175 million from BlackRock’s HPS to expand its AI compliance platform
blurb: Compliance is becoming a frontier AI market, and the capital is following. Behavox raised $175M in preferred equity from HPS, the private-credit arm BlackRock bought for $12B last year, its first equity raise since SoftBank in 2020. The pitch is consolidation: banks already run AI to catch misconduct, but most operate siloed systems per function, and Behavox unifies surveillance, archiving, and policy on one data layer. Ten of the 24 globally systemic banks now use it, with customer count up 86% in 2025. A private-credit giant backing regtech over a trading tool signals where institutional money sees the next AI edge.

title: 3 ex-Palantir engineers raise $60m from Index, Iconiq, SAP for agentic operating system
blurb: Conduct, founded in 2024 by three ex-Palantir engineers, raised $60M in Series A led by Index and Iconiq less than nine months after its $11M seed. The London startup builds an agentic AI operating system that synthesizes the decades of custom business logic buried across enterprise platforms, so teams can modify it in minutes instead of months of reverse-engineering code. SAP is embedding Conduct in its products and invested strategically, and customers already include DHL and Fraport, with the stated ambition to run the software of the Global 200.

title: The IPO Onslaught Is Forcing Bankers to Pick Teams
blurb: Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley are standing up walled-off teams to handle the OpenAI and Anthropic IPOs, so the same bank can underwrite both without leaking one client's information to the other. The wave of AI listings is large enough that the top underwriters now have to split their own staff to avoid conflicts. For founders, it signals that the biggest AI labs are moving toward public markets, and the bankers are already treating those debuts as the defining deals of the cycle.

title: The founder's playbook: Building an AI-native startup
blurb: Anthropic released a founder's playbook that remaps the four core startup stages — Idea, MVP, Launch, and Scale — for what AI makes possible in 2026, with goals, exit criteria, failure modes, and AI-powered exercises for each. The throughline is that the founder's job shifts from individual contributor to orchestrator, with non-technical founders shipping production apps and hitting revenue before scaling headcount. It is written for founders deciding how to architect the company around AI from day one, plus the early operators helping them get there.

title: Midjourney Medical
blurb: Midjourney, the bootstrapped AI image generator, launched a medical division built around a full-body Ultrasonic CT scanner that images the body in 60 seconds using sound waves and water instead of radiation. The company plans to open its first Midjourney Spa in San Francisco in 2027 and scale to 50,000 scanners running a billion scans a month by 2031. The bet is that a generative-imaging company can carry its core competency from pixels into physical hardware and a regulated health market, funded off its own profits rather than venture money.

title: Odyssey raises $310M at $1.45B valuation to transform AI model simulation
blurb: Odyssey raised $310M at a $1.45B valuation to build world models, AI systems that learn physics, causality, and time well enough to simulate outcomes before an action is taken. Natural Capital led, with Amazon, AMD Ventures, GV, and EQT joining, alongside a deal that makes AWS Trainium silicon Odyssey's preferred training stack. World models are the foundation for physical AI, robotics, and autonomous vehicles, and the round signals that investors now see simulating reality, not just predicting tokens, as the next foundation-model frontier worth funding at unicorn prices.

title: Ineffable Intelligence and Google Cloud partner on new frontier AI lab
blurb: London-based Ineffable Intelligence picked Google Cloud as its preferred infrastructure partner for a new frontier lab, weeks after closing a $1.1B seed round, the largest in European history. The bet is on experience-based learning, where systems generate and learn from real-time experience rather than static datasets, which demands tightly coupled training and inference at massive scale. The deal signals where frontier capital is flowing: not just raw compute, but reinforcement-learning infrastructure built around a specific architectural thesis, with the cloud providers increasingly underwriting the labs they host.

title: FTC lawsuit reveals how subscription scam networks evade app store enforcement
blurb: Subscription scam networks have outgrown the app stores' ability to police them. The FTC's suit against Genesis Tech alleges a web of shell companies in Cyprus and Ukraine that ran fitness, PDF, and horoscope apps pulling nearly a quarter-billion dollars from 2023 to mid-2025, with $700M flowing through connected PayPal accounts in a single year. By constantly spinning up new corporate entities and merchant accounts, the operator dodged Apple and Google fraud monitoring for years, which means the enforcement gap is structural, not a one-off any single takedown closes.

title: PayPal Ventures folds: how $1.5B in cuts wiped out a 24-year bet on 14 unicorns
blurb: PayPal is shutting down PayPal Ventures, its 24-year-old corporate VC arm, as new CEO Enrique Lores cuts $1.5B in costs and 20% of the workforce. The unit backed more than 156 companies and still holds 61 active positions, including 14 unicorns like Plaid, Olo, and Tabby, and Jefferies is now shopping the remaining stakes on the secondary market. With Fidelity International Strategic Ventures closing six weeks earlier, the strategic edge corporate VCs once sold, distribution and integration on top of capital, is looking like the first thing to get cut when the parent company tightens.

title: ASML CEO Sees Musk’s Terafab as Test for Supply Lines
blurb: ASML CEO Christophe Fouquet says Elon Musk's Terafab is a stress test for the chip supply chain, warning the equipment maker has to avoid supply constraints as projects of that scale come online. ASML is the sole maker of the EUV lithography machines every leading-edge fab depends on, so its capacity sets the ceiling on how fast anyone can build. The signal for founders building on frontier compute is that the bottleneck is moving upstream, from chip design to the machines and supply lines that physically produce the silicon.

title: Amazon AI exec predicts first 'commercially useful' quantum computers in 5-7 years
blurb: Amazon's top AI executive Peter DeSantis told CNBC the first commercially useful quantum computers will arrive in five to seven years, then scale year over year in a pattern resembling Moore's Law. The forecast lands in the middle of the field, more aggressive than Nvidia's Jensen Huang, who has called even 15 years optimistic, and roughly in line with Google and Microsoft. Quantum won't be a faster general computer; it targets a narrow class of problems classical machines handle poorly, so the near-term payoff stays confined to specialized workloads rather than mainstream compute.

title: SpaceX adds longtime Elon Musk ally Roelof Botha to board
blurb: A week after SpaceX's record IPO, Musk added Roelof Botha, his longtime ally and former Sequoia managing director, as an independent director on the audit committee. The "independent" label does a lot of lifting here, since Musk controls more than 82% of voting rights, chairs the board, and runs the company as CEO and tech chief, leaving outside shareholders almost no leverage. This is the founder-control playbook taken to its limit, where public markets buy the upside but governance stays inside the founder's circle.

title: South Korean and Taiwanese tech companies that helped build China's hardware sector during the smartphone boom now reap the AI boom as US curbs sideline China (New York Times)
blurb: South Korean and Taiwanese suppliers that built China's hardware base during the smartphone boom are now the biggest winners of the AI boom, as US export curbs sideline Chinese rivals. Companies like SK Hynix, TSMC, and Samsung are capturing the memory, packaging, and fabrication demand that flows to whoever sits outside the restrictions. The lesson for founders is that supply-chain position beats technology ownership when geopolitics redraws the map, and the firms that quietly own a critical layer often collect more than the ones chasing the frontier.

title: Robotic teleoperation data startup XDOF launches with $70M in funding
blurb: XDOF launched with $70M from Thrive, Spark, a16z, Lux, and WndrCo to build the data pipelines that teach robots how to act in the physical world. The bet is that physical AI's bottleneck isn't models or chips but the scarce, high-quality interaction data robots need, so XDOF also released ABC-130K, which it calls the largest open-source bimanual manipulation dataset. The timing tracks a broader shift, with OpenAI reviving its robotics program weeks earlier, and it points to data infrastructure as the next contested layer in robotics.

title: Conduct raises $60M to speed up software modernization projects
blurb: Conduct AI raised $60M in a Series A co-led by Index Ventures and ICONIQ, with SAP joining, to scale its AI platform for modernizing legacy enterprise code. The pitch targets the unglamorous backlog every large company carries: decades of custom modifications few employees still understand, with thin documentation, where ripping out one file can quietly break a critical feature. Conduct maps which custom modules a change touches, generates implementation plans and code, and automates the fit-gap analysis that makes years-long SAP S/4HANA migrations so slow, betting that AI modernization is where enterprise budgets move next.

title: AI material discovery startup CuspAI reportedly raising $400M round
blurb: CuspAI is reportedly raising $400M at a $2.6B valuation, more than five times its last round, with Bezos Expeditions and Kleiner Perkins in and Geoffrey Hinton and Yann LeCun advising. The UK startup runs an AI platform that generates material designs and tests them in simulation, cutting a process that traditionally takes years, and already counts ASML, Meta, and Hyundai as customers. The money chasing AI is moving past chatbots into the physical layer, where a working model means new conductors, batteries, and chip materials rather than another wrapper on someone else's API.

title: Apple investors are running out of patience with its AI promises
blurb: Apple investors are losing patience with its AI story, and the stock is coming off its worst week since February after a WWDC that moved no analyst to raise 2027 or 2028 estimates. Apple trades at more than 33 times forward earnings, well above its 23 long-run average, a premium that assumes an AI-driven iPhone upgrade cycle promised since 2024 and repeatedly delayed. The rebuilt Siri ships this autumn as a beta running on Google's Gemini and Nvidia GPUs, and it launches without the EU or China, two of Apple's largest markets.

title: Barcelona-based NeuralTrust raises €17.2 million to secure and govern enterprise AI agents
blurb: NeuralTrust raised €17.2M ($20M) in what it calls the largest cybersecurity Seed round by an EU company, led by Alstin Capital, to secure and govern enterprise AI agents. The Barcelona startup is betting on a gap that's widening fast: Gartner expects 40% of enterprises to demote or decommission autonomous agents by 2027 over governance failures found only after production incidents, yet just 13% of firms feel ready to manage them. As Fortune 500 companies head toward 150,000 agents each by 2028, the unglamorous work of knowing which agents exist and what they're allowed to do is becoming its own market.

title: Ex-Palantir team behind Conduct raises €51 million to make enterprise systems AI-ready
blurb: Conduct, founded by ex-Palantir engineers, raised €51M ($60M) in a Series A co-led by Index Ventures and ICONIQ, with strategic backing from SAP. The bet is that decades of customization have made enterprise systems like SAP, Salesforce, and Oracle too opaque for anyone to fully understand, and an agent can only act on a system it can read. Conduct maps the business logic buried in that customization, and customers including Daimler Truck, DHL, and Fraport report 30% or more acceleration in transformation work. The unglamorous bottleneck for enterprise AI is legibility, not models.

title: Former Palantir employees raise $60M for AI enterprise startup Conduct
blurb: Three former Palantir employees raised $60M in Series A funding for Conduct, a London startup that makes opaque legacy ERP systems legible to both humans and AI agents. The round was co-led by Index Ventures and Iconiq with a strategic investment from SAP, whose own ERP software Conduct's tools analyze and help companies migrate. The bet is that agents can only act on systems they understand, and decades of customization have made most enterprise ERP incomprehensible even to the people running it, so the unglamorous work of making those systems readable becomes the gate on every AI deployment.

title: CuspAI to hit $2.6bn valuation with backing from Jeff Bezos, reports say
blurb: CuspAI is raising $400m at a $2.6bn valuation, with Jeff Bezos's family office and Kleiner Perkins joining a round that more than quadruples the $100m mark it set last September. The Cambridge startup runs what amounts to a search engine for materials, with one set of AI models proposing new molecules for uses like sustainable energy and AI compute and another set predicting how they'd perform in the real world. Investors are paying up for AI applied to a hard science problem, not another software wrapper, and a board with Yann LeCun and Geoffrey Hinton is part of what they're buying.

title: French battlefield software startup Comand AI raises €32m from Blossom Capital, Saab
blurb: Comand AI raised €32m in Series A funding led by Blossom Capital, with Swedish defence prime Saab and Polish investor Expeditions joining. The Paris startup builds an AI platform that helps military officers plan, run, and analyze operations, with a stated goal of conducting them four times faster with four times fewer people. The platform is already deployed with units in France, Germany, and Ukraine, and the round funds expansion across NATO markets and into air and maritime domains, as European defence software draws sharper investor attention.

title: Mistral CEO pitches open source AI days after Anthropic ban: ‘We exist outside of state control’
blurb: Mistral is moving to fill the gap left after the US government ordered Anthropic to cut foreign nationals off from two of its best models, with CEO Arthur Mensch pitching open-source AI that "exists outside of state control." The structural argument is sovereignty: open weights let European states and companies inspect, customize, and run models on their own infrastructure, while closed US providers can have access revoked by Washington overnight. Mistral is still the small player, with €3.5bn raised and a reported €20bn round in talks against Anthropic's $965bn, but access risk is now its sharpest selling point.

title: This French startup’s AI is already calling shots on battlefields. Now, it’s raised €32M to scale
blurb: Comand AI, a Paris startup whose AI command-and-control platform Prevail is already running with operational units in Ukraine, France, and Germany, raised €32M in a Series A led by Blossom Capital. Saab joined as a strategic backer, a sign that Europe's established defense industry would rather invest in AI-native command software than build it in-house. The bet is the decision layer that sits above sensors, drones, and weapons, a gap Helsing at $18B and Anduril at $61B left open, and the open question is whether a €32M startup can claim it before the giants move down into it.

title: After taking Nvidia’s money, Odyssey raises $310M and bets on Amazon and AMD instead
blurb: Odyssey raised $310M at a $1.45B valuation to build general-purpose world models, with Amazon and AMD Ventures joining a round that names AWS as preferred cloud and hands the lab Amazon's Trainium chips. The pointed part is the timing, since Nvidia's venture arm backed Odyssey's Series A just four months ago, and the startup is now wiring its future compute to Nvidia's challengers instead. With 55 employees and only $27M raised before this, Odyssey is one of the most capital-intensive AI bets per head on the market.

title: Deutsches Roboter-Startup sammelt 1,4 Milliarden Dollar ein
blurb: Neura Robotics, a German humanoid-robot startup, raised up to $1.4B in a Series C from Amazon, Nvidia, Qualcomm, Bosch, Schaeffler, Tether, and the European Investment Bank, which it says is the largest round ever for a full-stack robotics company. The money goes into manufacturing and its "Neuraverse" platform, where robots are meant to share learned skills, with a stated goal of producing several million units by 2030 against a current order book above $1B. It positions Europe as a credible challenger to the US and Chinese players racing to own physical AI.

title: Sixty percent of US consumers say 'AI' in brand messaging is a turnoff
blurb: Brands have spent two years and real budget chasing AI brand visibility, yet 61% of US consumers can't name a single company doing it well and 60% say "AI" in brand messaging is a turnoff rather than a feature. The deeper problem is trust: 74% say the web feels less human than a decade ago, and bot fatigue sets in after about 40 minutes. The category has no incumbent and no template, so the first brand to feed AI engines clean data while still giving humans a reason to stay gets to define the standard.

title: PayPal Ventures shutters as company restructuring continues
blurb: PayPal is winding down PayPal Ventures, its corporate venture arm since 2016, pausing new investments and hiring Jefferies to explore secondary sales of holdings that include Plaid, Anchorage Digital, and Talos Global. The move is part of CEO Enrique Lores' restructuring push to "recommit to the fundamentals" and become a technology company again, with more cuts expected over the next few years. The risk is losing the front-row seat to fintech innovation that the fund provided, leaving PayPal blind to emerging startups just as rivals keep their venture arms running.

title: NeuralTrust, which offers tech that lets enterprises discover, monitor, govern, and secure AI agents, raised a $20M seed led by Alstin Capital (Tamara Djurickovic/Tech.eu)
blurb: NeuralTrust raised a $20M seed led by Alstin Capital to help enterprises discover, monitor, govern, and secure the AI agents now spreading through their stacks. The round signals where the money is moving: not toward building more agents, but toward controlling the ones already deployed, where an unmonitored agent with system access is a liability nobody has fully priced. As companies push agents into production faster than their security teams can track them, the governance layer is becoming its own category, and the early movers are getting funded.

title: Blackstone’s AirTrunk Seeks $3 Billion Loan for Sydney Project
blurb: Blackstone-backed AirTrunk is in talks with banks for a A$4.3 billion ($3 billion) loan to fund a new Sydney data center, extending its debt-fueled buildout across Asia Pacific. The scale of the borrowing shows how much of the AI infrastructure race is now financed on leverage rather than equity, with operators racing to lock in capacity before demand catches up. For founders, it signals that compute capex is consolidating into a few heavily capitalized landlords, and the cost of that debt will eventually flow through to the price of inference.

title: Two Stanford grads raise $11M to build a noninvasive wearable for hormone tracking
blurb: Clair Health raised $11.6M led by Khosla Ventures to build a noninvasive wearable that tracks hormones rather than the steps and heart rate today's smartwatches measure. The pitch is that Apple Watch and Pixel sensors miss hormonal health entirely, so the company built its own sensor stack plus an AI voice layer that classifies a user's cycle phase after a few minutes of conversation. The bet is that women's health data, captured continuously across all four cycle phases and shareable with providers, becomes a wedge into perimenopause and menopause care that generic wearables can't reach.

title: China AI Lab’s 170% Stock Surge Cements Winner-Loser Pair Trade
blurb: Chinese AI stocks are now trading as a pair, with investors crowding into the perceived race winner after a 170% surge while shorting the rival they see falling behind. The market has stopped treating China's AI field as one rising tide and started picking who actually ships commercially viable models. For founders, it is a reminder that capital is moving past the "everyone wins" phase of AI and toward separating the labs with a real product path from the ones still burning compute to catch up.

title: BWX Agrees to License Nuclear Reactor Design After Activist Push
blurb: BWX Technologies agreed to license its small modular reactor design after activist investor Ananym Capital Management pushed the company to commercialize the technology rather than keep it in-house. The move signals that SMRs are crossing from defense and R&D programs into licensable commercial assets, with an outside shareholder forcing the timeline. For founders building in energy or compute-heavy sectors, it marks nuclear shifting from a long-horizon bet to a near-term supply question, as activist pressure starts treating reactor IP like any other underused asset on the balance sheet.

title: Can Musk Pull Off a SpaceX-Tesla Merger?
blurb: Bloomberg Opinion columnist Liam Denning makes the case for folding SpaceX and Tesla into a single company, then lands on why it would be hard to do cleanly. The deal would pull a private rocket business and a public automaker under one roof, and the conflicts of interest run deep enough that minority Tesla shareholders would have a real claim to object. The harder question for founders watching is governance: when one founder controls multiple entities that increasingly share capital, talent, and technology, the line between synergy and self-dealing gets thin.

title: Goldman Tops $1 Trillion in M&A Deals, Fastest to Reach the Mark
blurb: Goldman Sachs has advised on more than $1 trillion of M&A so far in 2026, the fastest any bank has ever hit the mark and a sign the deal market has come roaring back. A handful of megadeals are doing the heavy lifting, which means the pace depends on a few large transactions holding rather than broad activity across the middle market. For founders, an open M&A window at the top usually signals more exit and acquisition appetite working its way down the stack.

title: AI Health Startup Wants to Assist Half of Latin American Doctors
blurb: An a16z-backed healthcare startup born in Latin America wants its AI assistant in the hands of half the region's 1.9 million doctors by the end of 2027. The bet is that software can stretch a thin supply of physicians across health systems that have far more patients than they can see. If it lands, it's a template for AI distribution in markets where the constraint isn't capital or talent but raw headcount, and where an assistant that makes one doctor more productive compounds faster than anywhere else.

title: Allbirds continues AI pivot with name change and CEO hire, sending stock soaring
blurb: Allbirds is changing its name to Smartbird and naming Nadia Carlsten, formerly head of AWS's quantum computing center and CEO of Nvidia-partnered DCAI, as its new chief executive, two months after pivoting from shoes to AI infrastructure. Shares jumped 39% on the news, extending a run that sevenfolded the market cap after April's rebrand. The pattern echoes the crypto-mania pivots of the last cycle, and a few of those worked: CoreWeave started as a crypto miner. The market is rewarding the AI label fast, before any compute revenue exists to back it.

title: Trump Says Anthropic Negotiations Continue as AI Leaders Huddle at G-7
blurb: Trump told reporters at the G-7 summit that talks with Anthropic are "going fine," even as the model shutdown that triggered them drags into its second week. The friction matters because some world leaders showed up worried about losing access to frontier AI tools their own governments and companies now depend on. For founders, the signal is that access to leading models has become a geopolitical variable, not a settled utility, and a single negotiation can throttle the infrastructure your product sits on.

title: How to Pivot Well
blurb: Knowing when to pivot, how fast, and toward what has quietly become a core founder skill in the AI era, since shifting model capabilities make a new pivot theoretically possible every quarter. A good pivot keeps something planted, usually the team and the investor's cash, while everything else moves, and the worst failure often comes from refusing to pivot at all once it's clear the current bet is wrong. Speedrun's investors argue inexperienced founders pivot best because they have the least to unlearn and aren't boxed into one market.

title: Apple Reveals Plans to Raise Prices
blurb: Apple revealed plans to raise prices on its next iPhone lineup, breaking from years of holding the entry tier flat. The move lands as tariffs and higher component costs squeeze hardware margins across the industry, and Apple is choosing to pass that cost to buyers rather than absorb it. For founders selling physical or premium-priced products, it signals that the market leader thinks pricing power still holds, and that customers will tolerate increases when the brand and switching costs are strong enough.

title: Convey closes on $38M round to help companies automate repetitive work with AI teammates
blurb: Convey raised $38M in a Series A led by Andreessen Horowitz, with Khosla Ventures and Pear VC, to let nontechnical workers build and run autonomous AI teammates with no code. The pitch is a bet against the copilot model, which lifts productivity at the margins but leaves the mundane back-office work (pulling spreadsheet data, processing invoices, reconciling financials) on the employee. With global AI spend projected past $2.5 trillion this year and most enterprises still seeing little back-office payoff, the wager is that agents that own a task outright, deployable in about three hours, are what actually moves the number.

title: Pramaana Labs raises $27M to make AI prove its answers
blurb: Pramaana Labs raised $27M in seed funding for a system that checks an AI model's answer against a domain's formal rules and refuses to return it unless a proof engine can verify it's correct. The product targets regulated work where mistakes are expensive, starting with tax, medical diagnosis, cybersecurity, and financial compliance, and the company says it has yet to produce a confidently wrong verified answer. The bet is that LLMs can write an answer that sounds right but can't show it's right, and in rule-bound fields that proof is the part that matters.

title: Why we're bullish on loops
blurb: PostHog argues you should stop prompting agents and instead build self-prompting loops with a goal, context, evaluation, and an agent.

title: FTC freezes sprawling subscription enterprise built on Cyprus and Delaware shells
blurb: A federal court temporarily shut down Genesis Tech, a subscription-app enterprise the FTC says ran 15 corporations and a roster of unrelated-looking apps, from fitness trackers to a psychic-chat horoscope app, on a single deceptive playbook. The structure was the point: affiliates incorporated in Cyprus and operating in Ukraine marketed to Americans while payment processing routed through Delaware shells, and each time fraud monitors flagged a merchant account, the operators opened a fresh one and moved the money across borders. Five products alone pulled in nearly $250M from 2023 to mid-2025, with connected PayPal accounts clearing close to $700M in a single year.

title: AI operating system startup Conduct closes £45m round
blurb: Conduct raised $60M in a Series A co-led by Index Ventures and ICONIQ, with strategic backing from SAP, to build an "AI operating system" for the bloated, heavily customized software that runs large enterprises. Decades of changes have left those systems opaque even to the people operating them, and that same opacity stops AI agents cold, because an agent can only act on a system it can understand. Conduct maps how every piece of custom code, config, and integration connects to the business logic it serves, betting that the unglamorous work of making legacy IT legible is where enterprise AI actually pays off.

title: AVP et Earlybird lancent fonds de 500 millions d'euros dédié à la défense
blurb: AVP and Earlybird are launching a €500M paneuropean defense fund, a deliberate break from the domestic €100M to €150M vehicles that dominate the European market. The French-German pairing was built to give portfolio companies access to Germany's military budget as Berlin moves to make up for decades of underinvestment, with AVP bringing software and AI and Earlybird bringing historical deep tech. The fund targets around 20 companies at roughly €25M per ticket in Series B and beyond, with a first close expected by the end of June.

title: Roelof Botha joins SpaceX’s board of directors
blurb: Roelof Botha joined SpaceX's board less than a week after the company's record-setting IPO, taking a seat on the audit committee alongside longtime Musk confidants. The former Sequoia managing partner brings public-company governance experience, but the board he joins has little real authority: Musk controls more than 80% of voting power and every change to the board's makeup. The signal for founders is that a marquee director hire doesn't always mean independent oversight, and in a founder-controlled company the board is there to advise, not to check the CEO.

title: Monument scores £18m seed for cloud banking platform
blurb: Monument Technology raised £18m in seed funding for its cloud Banking-Platform-as-a-Service, beating its initial £10m target. Founded in 2023, the UK firm stitches core banking, payments, and customer channels into one managed solution by integrating its own technology with existing components like Mambu, Salesforce, and Nice Actimize. The company has already opened a Series A at a higher valuation, a sign that demand for outsourced banking infrastructure is pulling capital forward even as financial institutions stay cautious about building these stacks in-house.

title: Dawn Aerospace Raises $25M Series B
blurb: Dawn Aerospace raised a $25M Series B at a $195M valuation to fund its Aurora spaceplane and in-orbit refueling demo.

title: Why ICONIQ led a $60M Series A for a London startup fixing SAP
blurb: Conduct, a 35-person London startup spun out of Palantir, raised a $60M Series A co-led by Index Ventures and ICONIQ, with SAP investing strategically. The bet is timing: mainstream support for SAP ECC ends December 31, 2027, and roughly 17,000 of 35,000 ECC customers still haven't migrated. Conduct maps the custom code, settings, and dependencies buried in core SAP systems back to their business purpose, so teams can ask what a change will touch before they make it. Customers like Daimler Truck, DHL, and Fraport report transformation projects moving at least 30% faster.

title: Europe Bets Industrial AI Can Salvage Its Manufacturing Edge
blurb: Europe is racing to put AI on the factory floor, with Mistral, Siemens, and Schneider Electric leading a push to make industrial production more efficient. The bet is that Europe's deep manufacturing base, rather than consumer chatbots, is where it can still out-compete the US and China. The open question is whether shop-floor AI can move fast enough to offset high energy costs and slow industrial output before the lead erodes.

title: Als eine US-Drohne versagt, überrascht ein Startup aus München im Kampf gegen russische Störsender
blurb: At a NATO electronic-warfare exercise in Lithuania, US units flooded the spectrum with jamming, yet Munich AI defense startup Helsing's HX-2 attack drone hit its target in 15 of 17 flights. When a US recon drone failed under the same jamming, American soldiers improvised and used the HX-2 to find targets too. That inverts a market that has run one way for decades, with Europe importing nearly all its flying hardware from the US. European defense startups now iterate against real Russian jamming alongside Ukrainian forces, and those combat-driven cycles are starting to beat US incumbents.

title: OpenAI kauft das Kieler KI-Startup Ona
blurb: OpenAI acquired Ona, the Kiel-based startup behind secure, reproducible cloud development environments, in what insiders call one of the largest German exits in years. The deal plugs a gap in Codex, OpenAI's coding assistant, which now has more than five million weekly users and grew 400% since January but needs a trusted place for agents to run multi-hour workflows inside a company's own infrastructure. For European founders, the read is that the frontier labs are now buying the operational layer that makes agents usable, and they are buying it from Europe.

title: Flagright secures $12.5M Series A to scale AI compliance platform
blurb: Flagright raised a $12.5M Series A led by Infinity Ventures to scale its AI operating system for financial crime compliance. The pitch is consolidation: one platform for transaction monitoring, watchlist screening, risk scoring, case management, and investigations, replacing the legacy systems and point solutions most institutions still stitch together. The differentiator is explainable AI, because regulated banks can only deploy models they can audit and defend, and the round goes toward expanding those use cases and pushing into the US market.

title: Next-gen nuclear company TerraPower plants flag in UK
blurb: TerraPower opened a UK subsidiary and started Step 1 of the country's Generic Design Assessment, its first move toward an international plant for its sodium-cooled Natrium reactor. The push tracks a broader nuclear resurgence driven by data center demand, electrification, and energy security, with the company already holding a Meta deal for up to eight reactors in the US. Next-gen nuclear is becoming a cross-border land grab, and the developers planting regulatory flags early are the ones positioned to supply the power-hungry compute buildout that everyone else is still scrambling to source.

title: Undo raises £27.6m to boost software issue diagnosis by AI agents
blurb: Cambridge-based Undo raised $37m (£27.6m) led by Elsewhere Partners to give AI coding agents the runtime context they need to diagnose complex bugs. Its deterministic recording technology captures complete execution history, so agents can see what a program actually did rather than just what the code says, sharpening root-cause analysis across dev, test, and production. The bet is that as AI writes more code, the bottleneck shifts to debugging it, and the tooling that makes AI-generated systems understandable becomes infrastructure rather than a nice-to-have.

title: At 21, Jenny Duan saw what Oura and WHOOP missed: women’s hormones. She raised $11.6M from Khosla Ventures to track them
blurb: Clair Health raised $11.6M led by Khosla to build the first noninvasive continuous hormone monitor for women, shipping November 2026.

title: Israeli Startup M&A in H1 2026: Strategic Buyers Are Back
